meta.title: Historical Figures
meta.subtitle: People who shaped the historical record — searchable.

glossary.heading: Roles — what each figure is chiefly known for

terms.Ruler: Monarchs, presidents, and heads of state.
terms.Explorer: Navigators and pioneers of unknown territory.
terms.Religious: Founders, reformers, and leaders of faith.
terms.Reformer: Activists and figures who drove social change.

meta.footer: A placeholder topic seeded with sample figures. Dates are scholarly estimates where exact dates are unknown.

entries.name: Hor-Aha
entries.desc: Hor-Aha is considered the second pharaoh of the First Dynasty of Egypt by most Egyptologists, while others consider him the first one and corresponding to Menes. He lived around the 31st century BC…
entries.tldr: Hor-Aha is considered the second pharaoh of the First Dynasty of Egypt by most Egyptologists, while others consider him the first one and corresponding to Menes. He lived around the 31st century BC and is thought to have had a long reign.

entries.name: Tutankhamun
entries.desc: Tutankhamun was the antepenultimate pharaoh of the Eighteenth Dynasty of ancient Egypt, who ruled c. 1332 – 1323 BC.

entries.name: King Wu of Zhou
entries.desc: Founding king of the Zhou dynasty who overthrew the Shang at the Battle of Muye and became a Confucian model of virtuous rule.

entries.name: Thespis
entries.desc: Thespis was a stage actor in Ancient Greece. According to certain Ancient Greek sources and especially Aristotle, he was the first human to appear on stage as an actor playing a character in a play.

entries.name: Pythagoras
entries.desc: Ancient Greek philosopher and mathematician who founded a religious-philosophical community and gave his name to fundamental theorems in mathematics.

entries.name: Confucius
entries.desc: Chinese teacher and philosopher whose ideas on ethics and governance shaped East Asian thought.

entries.name: Callicrates
entries.desc: Ancient Greek architect who co-designed the Parthenon in Athens alongside Ictinus.

entries.name: Praxiteles
entries.desc: Athenian sculptor of the 4th century BCE renowned for the Aphrodite of Knidos, the first monumental nude female figure in Western art.

entries.name: Demosthenes
entries.desc: Demosthenes was a Greek statesman and orator in ancient Athens. His orations provide insight into the politics and culture of ancient Greece during the 4th century BC.

entries.name: Darius III
entries.desc: Last Achaemenid King of Kings of Persia (336–330 BCE), defeated by Alexander the Great at Issus and Gaugamela.

entries.name: Menander
entries.desc: Menander was a Greek playwright and the best-known representative of Athenian New Comedy. He wrote 108 comedies and took the prize at the Lenaia festival eight times.

entries.name: Pushyamitra Shunga
entries.desc: Pushyamitra Shunga was the founder and the first ruler of the Shunga Empire which he established to succeed the Maurya Empire.

entries.name: Antigonus III Doson
entries.desc: Antigonus III Doson was king of Antigonid Macedon from 229 BC to 221 BC. Antigonus was a member of the Antigonid dynasty through his paternal line.
entries.tldr: Antigonus III Doson was king of Antigonid Macedon from 229 BC to 221 BC. During the Cleomenean War, Antigonus revived the Hellenic League of Philip II of Macedon under the name of the “League of Leagues” and managed to incorporate most of the Greek city-states in this League.

entries.name: Antigonus III Doson
entries.desc: Antigonus III Doson was king of Antigonid Macedon from 229 BC to 221 BC. Antigonus was a member of the Antigonid dynasty through his paternal line.
entries.tldr: Antigonus III Doson was king of Antigonid Macedon from 229 BC to 221 BC. During the Cleomenean War, Antigonus revived the Hellenic League of Philip II of Macedon under the name of the "League of Leagues" and managed to incorporate most of the Greek city-states in this League.

entries.name: Julius Caesar
entries.desc: Roman general and statesman whose rise ended the Republic and led to the Empire.

entries.name: Cornelius Nepos
entries.desc: Cornelius Nepos was a Roman biographer.
entries.tldr: Cornelius Nepos was a Roman biographer.

entries.name: Cornelius Nepos
entries.desc: Cornelius Nepos was a Roman biographer.
entries.tldr: Cornelius Nepos was a Roman biographer.

entries.name: Lepidus
entries.desc: Roman general who formed the Second Triumvirate with Octavian and Mark Antony after Caesar’s assassination, later sidelined as Pontifex Maximus.

entries.name: Cleopatra VII
entries.desc: Last active ruler of Ptolemaic Egypt, allied with Julius Caesar and Mark Antony.

entries.name: Tibullus
entries.desc: Albius Tibullus was a Latin poet and writer of elegies. His first and second books of poetry are extant; many other texts attributed to him are of questionable origins.
entries.tldr: Albius Tibullus was a Latin poet and writer of elegies. His first and second books of poetry are extant; many other texts attributed to him are of questionable origins.

entries.name: Tibullus
entries.desc: Albius Tibullus was a Latin poet and writer of elegies. His first and second books of poetry are extant; many other texts attributed to him are of questionable origins.
entries.tldr: Albius Tibullus was a Latin poet and writer of elegies. His first and second books of poetry are extant; many other texts attributed to him are of questionable origins.

entries.name: Marcus Gavius Apicius
entries.desc: Marcus Gavius Apicius is believed to have been a Roman gourmet and lover of luxury, who lived sometime in the 1st century AD. The Roman cookbook Apicius is often attributed to him.

entries.name: Sejanus
entries.desc: Lucius Aelius Sejanus was a Roman soldier and confidant of the Roman Emperor Tiberius. He rose to power as prefect of the Praetorian Guard and was executed for treason in AD 31.

entries.name: Suetonius
entries.desc: Roman historian of the early Empire, best known for The Twelve Caesars, biographies of Rome's first rulers.

entries.name: Soter
entries.desc: 2nd-century Pope of Rome who strengthened ties between the Roman church and Greek Christian communities.

entries.name: Gallienus
entries.desc: Roman emperor (253–268) who held the empire together during the Crisis of the Third Century and was a noted patron of philosophy and the arts.

entries.name: Arius
entries.desc: Alexandrian presbyter whose theology on Christ's nature sparked the Arian controversy, prompting the Council of Nicaea in 325 CE.

entries.name: Marcellus I
entries.desc: Pope from 308 to 309 CE, successor to Marcellinus; banished from Rome by Maxentius over disputes about Christian penance.

entries.name: Ulfilas
entries.desc: Ulfilas (Ancient Greek: Οὐλφίλας; c. 311 – 383), known also as Wulfila(s) or Urphilas, was a 4th-century Gothic preacher of Cappadocian Greek descent.
entries.tldr: Ulfilas (Ancient Greek: Οὐλφίλας; c. 311 – 383), known also as Wulfila(s) or Urphilas, was a 4th-century Gothic preacher of Cappadocian Greek descent. He was the apostle to the Gothic people.

entries.name: Huiyuan
entries.desc: Huiyuan refers to Lushan Huiyuan (334–416), a Chinese Buddhist monk.

entries.name: Samudragupta
entries.desc: Second emperor of the Gupta Empire who expanded India's territory through military conquest.

entries.name: Hypatia
entries.desc: Hypatia was a Neoplatonist philosopher, astronomer, and mathematician who lived in Alexandria. In Alexandria, Hypatia was a prominent thinker who taught subjects including philosophy and astronomy.

entries.name: Mesrop Mashtots
entries.desc: Armenian monk and linguist who created the Armenian alphabet around 405 CE, enabling the translation of the Bible and founding Armenian literature.

entries.name: Julius Nepos
entries.desc: Roman emperor of the West (474–475) who was deposed by Orestes but retained recognition from the East until his murder in 480.

entries.name: Odoacer
entries.desc: Germanic chieftain who deposed Romulus Augustulus in 476 and ruled Italy until overthrown by Theodoric the Great.

entries.name: Theodoric the Great
entries.desc: King of the Ostrogoths who conquered Italy in 493 and ruled as a tolerant monarch, promoting classical culture and coexistence of Romans and Goths.

entries.name: Alaric II
entries.desc: King of the Visigoths from 484 who issued the Breviary of Alaric, a landmark codification of Roman law for his subjects.

entries.name: Boethius
entries.desc: Roman senator, philosopher, and polymath of the Early Middle Ages whose Consolation of Philosophy became a cornerstone of medieval thought.

entries.name: Athalaric
entries.desc: King of the Ostrogoths in Italy from 526, grandson of Theodoric the Great, who reigned under the regency of his mother Amalasuintha.

entries.name: Ammar ibn Yasir
entries.desc: Companion of the Prophet Muhammad whose parents were the first martyrs of Islam; later a commander in the early Muslim conquests.

entries.name: Agatho
entries.desc: Pope (678–681) who presided over the Third Council of Constantinople, decisively condemning Monothelitism.

entries.name: Sa’id ibn Zayd
entries.desc: Companion of the Prophet Muhammad and early Muslim military leader.

entries.name: Muadh ibn Jabal
entries.desc: Companion of Muhammad who helped compile the Quran and became a leading Islamic scholar.

entries.name: Leo II
entries.desc: Pope (682–683) who ratified the Third Council of Constantinople, condemning Monothelitism and rehabilitating orthodox theology.

entries.name: Dagobert I
entries.desc: Dagobert I was King of the Franks. He ruled Austrasia (623–634) and Neustria and Burgundy (629–639). He has been described as the last king of the Merovingian dynasty to wield real royal power.

entries.name: Dagobert I
entries.desc: Dagobert I was King of the Franks. He ruled Austrasia (623–634) and Neustria and Burgundy (629–639). He has been described as the last king of the Merovingian dynasty to wield real royal power.

entries.name: Husayn ibn Ali
entries.desc: Grandson of the Prophet Muhammad and third Imam of Shia Islam; his death at the Battle of Karbala became the central tragedy of Shia faith.

entries.name: Sisinnius
entries.desc: Pope for about three weeks in 708, one of the shortest pontificates in history.

entries.name: Gregory II
entries.desc: Pope (715–731) who condemned Byzantine iconoclasm and supported Boniface’s missionary work among the Germanic peoples.

entries.name: Gregory III
entries.desc: Pope (731–741) who condemned Byzantine iconoclasm and cemented the papacy’s ties with the Frankish kingdom.

entries.name: Adrian I
entries.desc: Pope (772–795) who forged a lasting alliance with Charlemagne, shaping the political foundations of medieval Christendom.

entries.name: Paul I
entries.desc: Pope (757–768) who deepened the papacy’s alliance with the Frankish king Pepin the Short against the Lombards.

entries.name: Ibn al-Muqaffa'
entries.desc: Persian-born author and translator who rendered Kalila wa Dimna into Arabic, becoming a foundational figure of classical Arabic prose.

entries.name: Charlemagne
entries.desc: King of the Franks who united much of western Europe and was crowned Emperor in 800.

entries.name: Nikephoros I of Constantinople
entries.desc: Nikephoros I was a Byzantine writer and Ecumenical Patriarch of Constantinople from 12 April 806 to 13 March 815.

entries.name: Abu Ma’shar al-Balkhi
entries.desc: Persian astrologer and astronomer regarded as the greatest astrologer of the Abbasid court in Baghdad.

entries.name: Ansgar
entries.desc: Archbishop of Hamburg-Bremen known as the “Apostle of the North” for missionary journeys that brought Christianity to Scandinavia.

entries.name: Abd al-Rahman al-Sufi
entries.desc: Persian astronomer of the Islamic Golden Age whose Book of Fixed Stars gave the first recorded description of the Andromeda Galaxy.

entries.name: Sviatoslav I of Kiev
entries.desc: Prince of Kiev renowned for relentless military campaigns that toppled Khazaria and the First Bulgarian Empire while expanding Rus territory.

entries.name: Ibn al-Haytham
entries.desc: Arab mathematician and physicist from Iraq known as “the father of modern optics”; his Book of Optics transformed the science of vision.

entries.name: Henry II, Holy Roman Emperor
entries.desc: Holy Roman Emperor (1014–1024) and the last Ottonian emperor, later canonised for his support of Church reform.

entries.name: Al-Biruni
entries.desc: Abu Rayhan Muhammad ibn Ahmad al-Biruni was a Khwarazmian Iranic scholar and polymath during the Islamic Golden Age. He has been called the "Father of Comparative Religion" and "Father of modern geodesy".

entries.name: Lady Godiva
entries.desc: Anglo-Saxon noblewoman and patron of churches, legendary for riding naked through Coventry to persuade her husband to lower taxes on the poor.

entries.name: Nicholas II
entries.desc: Nicholas II was Emperor of Russia from 1 November 1894 until his abdication in 1917. He was the last Russian monarch before the Russian Revolution.

entries.name: Abū Ishāq Ibrāhīm al-Zarqālī
entries.desc: Arab astronomer and instrument-maker from Al-Andalus who invented the saphaea and compiled the influential Toledo Tables.

entries.name: Lulach
entries.desc: Lulach mac Gille Coemgáin was King of Alba (Scotland) between 15 August 1057 and 17 March 1058.

entries.name: Henry I of England
entries.desc: Fourth son of William the Conqueror who seized the English throne in 1100 and stabilised the realm.

entries.name: Sanai
entries.desc: Persian Sufi poet of the Ghaznavid Empire who pioneered mystical verse in Persian literature.

entries.name: Avempace
entries.desc: Andalusian Arab polymath who wrote on astronomy, physics, philosophy, and medicine in Al-Andalus.

entries.name: Adam of Bremen
entries.desc: Adam of Bremen was a German medieval chronicler. He lived and worked in the second half of the eleventh century. Adam is most famous for his chronicle Gesta Hammaburgensis ecclesiae pontificum.

entries.name: Adam of Bremen
entries.desc: Adam of Bremen was a German medieval chronicler. He lived and worked in the second half of the eleventh century. Adam is most famous for his chronicle Gesta Hammaburgensis ecclesiae pontificum.

entries.name: Geoffrey Plantagenet, Count of Anjou
entries.desc: Count of Anjou and Duke of Normandy by conquest who founded the Plantagenet dynasty that would rule England for three centuries.

entries.name: Lu You
entries.desc: Lu You was a Chinese historian and poet of the Southern Song Dynasty (南宋).
entries.tldr: Lu You was a Chinese historian and poet of the Southern Song Dynasty (南宋).

entries.name: Henry II of England
entries.desc: King of England (1154–1189) who presided over the Angevin Empire, spanning England, much of France, and parts of Ireland and Wales.

entries.name: Saladin
entries.desc: First sultan of Egypt and Syria who led Muslim forces during the Crusades.

entries.name: Malcolm IV of Scotland
entries.desc: Malcolm IV, nicknamed Virgo, "the Maiden" was King of Scotland from 1153 until his death.

entries.name: Saxo Grammaticus
entries.desc: Saxo Grammaticus, also known as Saxo cognomine Longus, was a Danish historian, theologian, and author. He is thought to have been a clerk or secretary to Absalon, Archbishop of Lund, the main advis…
entries.tldr: Saxo Grammaticus, also known as Saxo cognomine Longus, was a Danish historian, theologian, and author. He is thought to have been a clerk or secretary to Absalon, Archbishop of Lund, the main advisor to Valdemar I of Denmark. He is the author of the Gesta Danorum, the first full history of Denmark, from which the legend of Amleth would come to inspire the story of Hamlet by Shakespeare.

entries.name: Sancho I of Portugal
entries.desc: Second King of Portugal, known as Sancho the Populator for rebuilding and repopulating towns reconquered from the Moors.

entries.name: Kuchlug
entries.desc: Kuchlug was a member of the Naiman tribe who became the last emperor of the Western Liao dynasty. The Naimans were defeated by Genghis Khan and he fled westward to the Western Liao, where he became…
entries.tldr: Kuchlug was a member of the Naiman tribe who became the last emperor of the Western Liao dynasty. The Naimans were defeated by Genghis Khan and he fled westward to the Western Liao, where he became an advisor to his future father-in-law Yelü Zhilugu. He later rebelled, usurped the throne and took control of the empire, putting an end to the rule of the House of Yelü.

entries.name: Philip of Swabia
entries.desc: Philip of Swabia was a member of the House of Hohenstaufen and King of Germany from 1198 until his assassination.

entries.name: Snorri Sturluson
entries.desc: Icelandic historian, poet, and twice-elected lawspeaker of the Althing, credited with compiling the Prose Edda and Heimskringla.

entries.name: Töregene Khatun
entries.desc: Töregene Khatun was the Great Khatun and regent of the Mongol Empire from the death of her husband Ögedei Khan in 1241 until the election of her eldest son Güyük Khan in 1246.
entries.tldr: Töregene Khatun was the Great Khatun and regent of the Mongol Empire from the death of her husband Ögedei Khan in 1241 until the election of her eldest son Güyük Khan in 1246.

entries.name: Nicholas IV
entries.desc: First Franciscan pope (1288–1292) who dispatched missionaries to Asia and sought Mongol-Christian alliances against the Mamluks.

entries.name: Dante Alighieri
entries.desc: Florentine poet whose Divine Comedy is widely regarded as the greatest literary work of the Middle Ages and the foundation of the Italian language.

entries.name: Malhun Hatun
entries.desc: Second wife of Osman I, founder of the Ottoman dynasty, whose family alliances helped consolidate the early Ottoman state.

entries.name: Ferdinand I of Portugal
entries.desc: Ferdinand I, sometimes called the Handsome or occasionally the Inconstant, was the King of Portugal from 1367 until his death in 1383. He was also briefly made King of Galicia, in 1369.
entries.tldr: Ferdinand I, sometimes called the Handsome or occasionally the Inconstant, was the King of Portugal from 1367 until his death in 1383. He was also briefly made King of Galicia, in 1369. Facing a lack of legitimate male heirs, his death led to the 1383–85 crisis, also known as the Portuguese interregnum.

entries.name: Ferdinand I of Portugal
entries.desc: Ferdinand I, sometimes called the Handsome or occasionally the Inconstant, was the King of Portugal from 1367 until his death in 1383. He was also briefly made King of Galicia, in 1369.
entries.tldr: Ferdinand I, sometimes called the Handsome or occasionally the Inconstant, was the King of Portugal from 1367 until his death in 1383. He was also briefly made King of Galicia, in 1369. Facing a lack of legitimate male heirs, his death led to the 1383–85 crisis, also known as the Portuguese interregnum.

entries.name: Jamshīd al-Kāshī
entries.desc: Persian astronomer and mathematician who calculated pi to 16 decimal places under Timurid patronage.

entries.name: Musa Çelebi
entries.desc: Musa Çelebi was an Ottoman prince and a co-ruler of the empire for three years during the Ottoman Interregnum.
entries.tldr: Musa Çelebi was an Ottoman prince and a co-ruler of the empire for three years during the Ottoman Interregnum.

entries.name: Musa Çelebi
entries.desc: Musa Çelebi was an Ottoman prince and a co-ruler of the empire for three years during the Ottoman Interregnum.
entries.tldr: Musa Çelebi was an Ottoman prince and a co-ruler of the empire for three years during the Ottoman Interregnum.

entries.name: Jan van Eyck
entries.desc: Early Netherlandish painter active in Bruges, renowned as one of the supreme innovators of Early Northern Renaissance oil painting.

entries.name: Maria of Castile, Queen of Aragon
entries.desc: Queen of Aragon and longtime regent of the Crown, effectively governing Aragon for most of Alfonso V's reign while he campaigned in Italy.

entries.name: Pius II
entries.desc: Pope (1458–1464) and humanist scholar who called a failed crusade against the Ottoman Empire.

entries.name: Joan of Arc
entries.desc: French peasant who led armies during the Hundred Years' War and was later canonised.

entries.name: Paul II
entries.desc: Pope from 1464 to 1471, born Pietro Barbo of Venice; known for his love of art and gems and his conflicts with humanist scholars.

entries.name: Alexander VI
entries.desc: Pope Alexander VI was head of the Catholic Church and leader of the Papal States from 11 August 1492 until his death in 1503.
entries.tldr: Pope Alexander VI was head of the Catholic Church and leader of the Papal States from 11 August 1492 until his death in 1503.

entries.name: Hans Memling
entries.desc: Hans Memling was a German-Flemish painter who worked in the tradition of Early Netherlandish painting. Born in the Middle Rhine region, he probably spent his childhood in Mainz. During his apprenticeship as a painter he moved to the Netherlands and spent time in the Brussels workshop of Rogier van der Weyden.

entries.name: Ladislaus the Posthumous
entries.desc: Ladislaus the Posthumous, known as Ladislaus V, was Duke of Austria and King of Hungary, Croatia and Bohemia.

entries.name: Leonardo da Vinci
entries.desc: Italian polymath of the Renaissance, painter of the Mona Lisa and prolific inventor.

entries.name: Sri Krishnadevaraya
entries.desc: Krishnadevaraya was emperor of the Vijayanagara Empire from 1509 to 1529. Widely regarded as one of the greatest rulers in Indian history, he presided over the empire at its political and cultural zenith.

entries.name: Paul IV
entries.desc: Pope (1555–1559) who established the first Roman ghetto and intensified the Roman Inquisition.

entries.name: Ferdinand Magellan
entries.desc: Portuguese explorer who organised the first expedition to circumnavigate the globe.

entries.name: Martin Luther
entries.desc: German theologian whose Ninety-five Theses sparked the Protestant Reformation.

entries.name: Zhengde Emperor
entries.desc: 11th emperor of the Ming dynasty (1505–1521), known for unconventional rule and fondness for military pursuits.

entries.name: Clément Marot
entries.desc: Clément Marot was a French Renaissance poet. He was influenced by the writers of the late 15th century and paved the way for the Pléiade, and is undoubtedly the most important poet at the court of Francis I.

entries.name: Clément Marot
entries.desc: Clément Marot was a French Renaissance poet. He was influenced by the writers of the late 15th century and paved the way for the Pléiade, and is undoubtedly the most important poet at the court of Francis I.

entries.name: Longqing Emperor
entries.desc: 13th emperor of the Ming dynasty (1567–1572), whose brief reign brought pragmatic diplomatic and trade reforms.

entries.name: Carlo Maderno
entries.desc: Carlo Maderno was an Italian architect, born in today’s Ticino, Switzerland, who is remembered as one of the fathers of Baroque architecture.

entries.name: Michael the Brave
entries.desc: Michael the Brave was the Prince of Wallachia, Prince of Moldavia (1600) and de facto ruler of Transylvania (1599–1600). He is considered one of Romania's greatest national heroes.

entries.name: Katō Kiyomasa
entries.desc: Katō Kiyomasa  was a Japanese daimyō of the Azuchi–Momoyama and Edo periods. His court title was Higo no Kami .
entries.tldr: Katō Kiyomasa  was a Japanese daimyō of the Azuchi–Momoyama and Edo periods. His court title was Higo no Kami . His name as a child was Yashamaru, and first name was Toranosuke.

entries.name: Jan Pieterszoon Sweelinck
entries.desc: Dutch composer, organist, and pedagogue whose work bridged the Renaissance and Baroque eras; shaped the north German organ tradition.

entries.name: Wanli Emperor
entries.desc: 14th and longest-reigning Ming emperor (1572–1620), whose 48-year reign ended in prolonged political withdrawal.

entries.name: Michael Praetorius
entries.desc: Michael Praetorius was a German composer, organist, and music theorist. He was one of the most versatile composers of his age, being particularly significant in the development of musical forms bas…
entries.tldr: Michael Praetorius was a German composer, organist, and music theorist. He was one of the most versatile composers of his age, being particularly significant in the development of musical forms based on Protestant hymns.

entries.name: Innocent X
entries.desc: Pope (1644–1655) who condemned the Peace of Westphalia and was immortalized by Velázquez's portrait.

entries.name: Guido Reni
entries.desc: Guido Reni was an Italian Baroque painter, although his works showed a classical manner. He painted primarily religious works, but also mythological and allegorical subjects.

entries.name: Guido Reni
entries.desc: Guido Reni was an Italian Baroque painter, although his works showed a classical manner. He painted primarily religious works, but also mythological and allegorical subjects.

entries.name: Taichang Emperor
entries.desc: 15th Ming emperor whose reign lasted less than a month in 1620 before his sudden and mysterious death.

entries.name: Leopold V, Archduke of Austria
entries.desc: Habsburg archduke who served as Prince-Bishop of Passau and Strasbourg before assuming secular rule over Further Austria.

entries.name: Ahmed I
entries.desc: Ottoman sultan who commissioned the Blue Mosque in Istanbul and ruled the empire from 1603 to 1617.

entries.name: Anne Hutchinson
entries.desc: Anne Hutchinson was an English-born religious figure who was an important participant in the Antinomian Controversy which shook the nascent Massachusetts Bay Colony from 1636 to 1638.

entries.name: Francesco Cavalli
entries.desc: Francesco Cavalli was a Venetian composer, organist and singer of the early Baroque period. He succeeded his teacher Claudio Monteverdi as the dominant and leading opera composer of the mid 17th-ce…
entries.tldr: Francesco Cavalli was a Venetian composer, organist and singer of the early Baroque period. He succeeded his teacher Claudio Monteverdi as the dominant and leading opera composer of the mid 17th-century. A central figure of Venetian musical life, Cavalli wrote more than thirty operas, almost all of which premiered in the city's theaters.

entries.name: John IV of Portugal
entries.desc: First Braganza king, restored Portuguese independence from Spain in 1640; also a prolific composer and patron of music.

entries.name: Tianqi Emperor
entries.desc: 16th Ming emperor (1620–1627) who ceded court power to the eunuch Wei Zhongxian while pursuing carpentry.

entries.name: Christine of France
entries.desc: Christine of France was Duchess of Savoy from 26 July 1630 to 7 October 1637 as the consort of Duke Victor Amadeus I. She was the daughter of Henry IV of France and sister of Louis XIII.
entries.tldr: Christine of France was Duchess of Savoy from 26 July 1630 to 7 October 1637 as the consort of Duke Victor Amadeus I. She was the daughter of Henry IV of France and sister of Louis XIII. Following her husband's death in 1637, she acted as regent of Savoy between 1637 and 1648.

entries.name: Christine of France
entries.desc: Christine of France was Duchess of Savoy from 26 July 1630 to 7 October 1637 as the consort of Duke Victor Amadeus I. She was the daughter of Henry IV of France and sister of Louis XIII.
entries.tldr: Christine of France was Duchess of Savoy from 26 July 1630 to 7 October 1637 as the consort of Duke Victor Amadeus I. She was the daughter of Henry IV of France and sister of Louis XIII. Following her husband's death in 1637, she acted as regent of Savoy between 1637 and 1648.

entries.name: Evangelista Torricelli
entries.desc: Italian physicist and mathematician, student of Galileo, best known for inventing the barometer.

entries.name: Alexander VIII
entries.desc: Pope (1689–1691) known for nepotism and his condemnation of Jansenism during his brief pontificate.

entries.name: Chongzhen Emperor
entries.desc: Last emperor of the Ming dynasty (1627–1644), who hanged himself as rebel forces captured Beijing.

entries.name: Innocent XI
entries.desc: Pope (1676–1689) who led the Holy League against Ottoman expansion and championed Church reform.

entries.name: Saskia van Uylenburgh
entries.desc: Saskia van Uylenburgh was the wife of painter Rembrandt van Rijn. In the course of her life, she was his model for some of his paintings, drawings, and etchings.

entries.name: Saskia van Uylenburgh
entries.desc: Saskia van Uylenburgh was the wife of painter Rembrandt van Rijn. In the course of her life, she was his model for some of his paintings, drawings, and etchings.

entries.name: Empress Meishō
entries.desc: Empress Meishō was the 109th monarch of Japan. Her reign lasted from 1629 to 1643.

entries.name: John Bunyan
entries.desc: John Bunyan was an English writer and nonconformist preacher. He is best remembered as the author of the Christian allegory The Pilgrim's Progress, which also became an influential literary model.
entries.tldr: John Bunyan was an English writer and nonconformist preacher. He is best remembered as the author of the Christian allegory The Pilgrim's Progress, which also became an influential literary model. In addition to The Pilgrim's Progress, Bunyan wrote nearly sixty titles, many of them expanded sermons.

entries.name: Charles II of England
entries.desc: Charles II was King of Scotland from 1649 until 1651 and King of England, Scotland, and Ireland from the 1660 Restoration of the monarchy until his death in 1685.

entries.name: Jean-Baptiste Lully
entries.desc: Jean-Baptiste Lully was an Italian-French composer, dancer and instrumentalist, who is considered a master of the French Baroque music style. Best known for his operas, he spent most of his life working in the court of Louis XIV.

entries.name: Samuel Pepys
entries.desc: English naval administrator and MP whose candid diary, kept 1660–1669, is among the most vivid primary sources for the Stuart Restoration.

entries.name: Isaac Newton
entries.desc: English physicist and mathematician who formulated the laws of motion and gravitation.

entries.name: Denis Papin
entries.desc: French Huguenot physicist and inventor of the steam digester, forerunner of both the pressure cooker and the steam engine.

entries.name: Jacob Bernoulli
entries.desc: Jacob Bernoulli was a Swiss mathematician and an early proponent of Leibnizian calculus. Along with his brother Johann, he was one of the founders of the calculus of variations.

entries.name: Anne of Great Britain
entries.desc: Anne of Great Britain may refer to Anne, Queen of Great Britain (1665–1714), who ruled after William III and before the House of Hanover.

entries.name: Robert Walpole
entries.desc: British Whig statesman regarded as the de facto first Prime Minister of Great Britain, serving 1721–1742.

entries.name: Louis, Dauphin of France, Duke of Burgundy
entries.desc: Grandson of Louis XIV known as le Petit Dauphin, groomed by Fénelon to be an enlightened king but died of measles before acceding.

entries.name: Jean-Philippe Rameau
entries.desc: French Baroque composer and music theorist who became the dominant figure in French opera.

entries.name: Friedrich Wilhelm I of Prussia
entries.desc: King of Prussia (1713–1740) known as the ‘Soldier King’ who built a highly disciplined army and centralised the Prussian state.

entries.name: Giuseppe Tartini
entries.desc: Giuseppe Tartini was an Italian composer and violinist of the Baroque era born in Pirano in the Republic of Venice. Tartini was a prolific composer, composing over a hundred pieces for the violin, the majority of them violin concertos. He is best remembered for his Violin Sonata in G Minor.

entries.name: Giuseppe Tartini
entries.desc: Giuseppe Tartini was an Italian composer and violinist of the Baroque era born in Pirano in the Republic of Venice. He is best remembered for his Violin Sonata in G Minor.

entries.name: Pierre Bouguer
entries.desc: French mathematician, geophysicist, and astronomer known as “the father of naval architecture” and for pioneering photometry.

entries.name: Johann Jakob Bodmer
entries.desc: Johann Jakob Bodmer was a Swiss author, academic, critic and poet.

entries.name: Daniel Bernoulli
entries.desc: Daniel Bernoulli was a Swiss mathematician and physicist particularly remembered for his applications of mathematics to fluid mechanics and his pioneering work in probability and statistics.

entries.name: Gabriel Cramer
entries.desc: Gabriel Cramer was a Genevan mathematician.

entries.name: Carl Linnaeus
entries.desc: Swedish biologist who formalised binomial nomenclature and is known as the father of modern taxonomy.

entries.name: Wilhelm Friedemann Bach
entries.desc: Wilhelm Friedemann Bach was a German composer, organist and harpsichordist. He was the second child and eldest son of Johann Sebastian Bach and Maria Barbara Bach.
entries.tldr: Wilhelm Friedemann Bach was a German composer, organist and harpsichordist. He was the second child and eldest son of Johann Sebastian Bach and Maria Barbara Bach. Despite his acknowledged genius as an improviser and composer, his income and employment were unstable, and he died in poverty.

entries.name: José I of Portugal
entries.desc: Dom Joseph I, known as the Reformer, was King of Portugal from 1750 until his death in 1777, with his reign dominated by the Marquis of Pombal.

entries.name: Louis, Dauphin of France
entries.desc: Son of Louis XV of France and father of three kings (Louis XVI, Louis XVIII, Charles X) who never himself ascended the throne.

entries.name: Johann Christian Bach
entries.desc: Youngest son of J.S. Bach, German-born Classical composer who settled in London and profoundly influenced the young Mozart.

entries.name: John Adams
entries.desc: John Adams was a Founding Father and the second president of the United States from 1797 to 1801. He was a leader of the American Revolution that achieved independence from Great Britain.

entries.name: Horace Bénédict de Saussure
entries.desc: Horace Bénédict de Saussure was a Genevan geologist, meteorologist, physicist, mountaineer and Alpine explorer, often called the founder of alpinism and modern meteorology.

entries.name: Henry Fuseli
entries.desc: Henry Fuseli was a Swiss painter, draughtsman, and writer on art who spent much of his career in Britain.

entries.name: Thomas Jefferson
entries.desc: Thomas Jefferson was a Founding Father and the third president of the United States from 1801 to 1809. Jefferson was the primary author of the Declaration of Independence.

entries.name: Jean-Paul Marat
entries.desc: Jean-Paul Marat was a French political theorist, physician, and scientist. A journalist and politician during the French Revolution, he was a vigorous defender of the sans-culottes.

entries.name: Johann Heinrich Pestalozzi
entries.desc: Swiss educational reformer who pioneered child-centred learning methods emphasising hands-on experience, shaping the foundations of modern elementary education.

entries.name: Tipu Sultan
entries.desc: Sultan of Mysore (1782–1799) who pioneered iron-cased rocket artillery and fiercely resisted British expansion.

entries.name: James Madison
entries.desc: James Madison was an American statesman, diplomat, and Founding Father who served as the fourth president of the United States from 1809 to 1817. He was popularly acclaimed as the "Father of the Constitution".

entries.name: James Monroe
entries.desc: James Monroe was an American Founding Father who served as the fifth president of the United States from 1817 to 1825. He was the last Founding Father to serve as president.

entries.name: Mary Wollstonecraft
entries.desc: English writer and philosopher whose A Vindication of the Rights of Woman (1792) is a founding text of feminism.

entries.name: Johann Gottlieb Fichte
entries.desc: German philosopher and founding figure of German Idealism who extended Kant's ethics and epistemology into a systematic philosophy of the self.

entries.name: Andreas Hofer
entries.desc: Andreas Hofer was a Tyrolean innkeeper and drover who became the leader of the 1809 Tyrolean Rebellion during the War of the Fifth Coalition.

entries.name: John Quincy Adams
entries.desc: John Quincy Adams was the sixth president of the United States, serving from 1825 to 1829. He previously served as the eighth United States secretary of state from 1817 to 1825.

entries.name: Andrew Jackson
entries.desc: Andrew Jackson was the seventh president of the United States from 1829 to 1837. He rose to fame as a U.S. Army general.

entries.name: William Henry Harrison
entries.desc: William Henry Harrison was the ninth president of the United States from March to April 1841. He died 31 days into his term, making him the shortest serving president and the first president to die in office.

entries.name: Caspar David Friedrich
entries.desc: German Romantic painter whose landscapes, including Wanderer above the Sea of Fog, express spiritual solitude and the sublime power of nature.

entries.name: Giuseppe Caspar Mezzofanti
entries.desc: Giuseppe Gasparo Mezzofanti was an Italian cardinal known for being a hyperpolyglot. He spoke 38 to 50 languages.

entries.name: François Adrien Boieldieu
entries.desc: François-Adrien Boieldieu was a French composer, mainly of operas, often called “the French Mozart”.

entries.name: François Adrien Boieldieu
entries.desc: François-Adrien Boieldieu was a French composer, mainly of operas, often called "the French Mozart".

entries.name: Matthew Gregory Lewis
entries.desc: Matthew Gregory Lewis was an English novelist and dramatist whose writings are often classified as "Gothic horror". He was frequently referred to as "Monk" Lewis, because of his 1796 Gothic novel The Monk.

entries.name: Antoine-Henri Jomini
entries.desc: Baron Antoine-Henri Jomini was a Swiss-French military officer who served as a general in French and later in Russian service, and one of the most celebrated writers on the Napoleonic art of war.

entries.name: Richard Mentor Johnson
entries.desc: Richard Mentor Johnson was an American lawyer, military officer and politician who served as the ninth vice president of the United States from 1837 to 1841 under President Martin Van Buren.

entries.name: Richard Mentor Johnson
entries.desc: Richard Mentor Johnson was an American lawyer, military officer and politician who served as the ninth vice president of the United States from 1837 to 1841 under President Martin Van Buren.

entries.name: Daniel Webster
entries.desc: American lawyer and statesman who served as U.S. Senator and twice as Secretary of State, a dominant voice for Union and constitutional law.

entries.name: Martin Van Buren
entries.desc: Martin Van Buren was the eighth president of the United States, serving from 1837 to 1841. He co-founded the Democratic Party with Andrew Jackson.

entries.name: Zachary Taylor
entries.desc: Zachary Taylor was the 12th president of the United States, serving from 1849 until his death in 1850. He was a career officer in the United States Army, rising to major general.

entries.name: Alessandro Manzoni
entries.desc: Italian poet and novelist whose The Betrothed (1827) is considered the greatest Italian novel and a pillar of the Risorgimento.

entries.name: John Tyler
entries.desc: John Tyler was the tenth president of the United States, serving from 1841 to 1845. He succeeded to the presidency following Harrison's death 31 days after assuming office.

entries.name: Giacomo Meyerbeer
entries.desc: German-Jewish opera composer whose grand operas—including Les Huguenots—dominated Parisian stages for decades and influenced later Romantic opera.

entries.name: James K. Polk
entries.desc: James Knox Polk was the 11th president of the United States, serving from 1845 to 1849. He was an advocate of American expansionism and Jacksonian democracy.

entries.name: Horace Mann
entries.desc: American educational reformer who championed public schooling, known as the Father of American Education.

entries.name: Gaetano Donizetti
entries.desc: Italian composer of over 70 operas, a leading figure of the bel canto style alongside Rossini and Bellini.

entries.name: Sojourner Truth
entries.desc: American abolitionist born into slavery who delivered the landmark ‘Ain’t I a Woman?’ speech at the 1851 Women’s Rights Convention.

entries.name: Mirza Ghalib
entries.desc: Mirza Asadullah Beg Khan, commonly known as Mirza Ghalib, was an Indian poet and letter writer of the Mughal Empire. Writing in Persian and Urdu during the final years of the Mughal Empire and the …
entries.tldr: Mirza Asadullah Beg Khan, commonly known as Mirza Ghalib, was an Indian poet and letter writer of the Mughal Empire. Writing in Persian and Urdu during the final years of the Mughal Empire and the rise of British colonial rule, his poetry often addressed themes of love, loss, philosophy, the human condition, and socio-political disturbances with a depth and complexity that influenced the litera…

entries.name: Jeremias Gotthelf
entries.desc: Albert Bitzius was a Swiss novelist, best known by his pen name of Jeremias Gotthelf.

entries.name: Charles Wilkes
entries.desc: Charles Wilkes was an American naval officer, ship's captain, and explorer. He led the first United States Exploring Expedition (1838–1842).

entries.name: William Lassell
entries.desc: English merchant-astronomer who improved the reflecting telescope and discovered Triton, Ariel, Umbriel, and Hyperion.

entries.name: France Prešeren
entries.desc: Slovene Romantic poet whose sonnets and the poem Zdravljica established him as the national poet of Slovenia.

entries.name: Hector Berlioz
entries.desc: French Romantic composer known for Symphonie fantastique and his influential innovations in orchestration.

entries.name: Charles-Augustin Sainte-Beuve
entries.desc: Foremost French literary critic of the 19th century, influential for his biographical method of contextualizing authors' lives and works.

entries.name: William Rowan Hamilton
entries.desc: Sir William Rowan Hamilton was an Irish mathematician, physicist, and astronomer who made numerous major contributions to algebra, classical mechanics, and optics.

entries.name: Matthew Fontaine Maury
entries.desc: American oceanographer and naval officer who pioneered systematic charting of ocean winds and currents.

entries.name: Charles Darwin
entries.desc: English naturalist whose theory of evolution by natural selection transformed biology.

entries.name: Harriet Beecher Stowe
entries.desc: American author and abolitionist whose novel Uncle Tom's Cabin (1852) galvanized the anti-slavery movement across the US and Britain.

entries.name: Urbain Le Verrier
entries.desc: French astronomer who predicted the existence and position of Neptune through mathematics alone, prompting its discovery in 1846.

entries.name: Samuel Smiles
entries.desc: Samuel Smiles was a British author and government reformer. His primary work, Self-Help (1859), promoted thrift and claimed that poverty was caused largely by irresponsible habits.

entries.name: Ivar Andreas Aasen
entries.desc: Ivar Andreas Aasen was a Norwegian philologist, lexicographer, playwright, and poet. He is best known for having assembled one of the two official written versions of the Norwegian language, Nynors…
entries.tldr: Ivar Andreas Aasen was a Norwegian philologist, lexicographer, playwright, and poet. He is best known for having assembled one of the two official written versions of the Norwegian language, Nynorsk, from various dialects.

entries.name: Baháʼu'lláh
entries.desc: Iranian founder of the Baháʼí Faith who proclaimed a new divine revelation in 1863 after decades of exile.

entries.name: Báb
entries.desc: Iranian religious leader who founded Bábism in 1844 and is revered as a central figure of the Baháʼí Faith.

entries.name: Annibale de Gasparis
entries.desc: Italian astronomer who discovered nine asteroids and contributed to theoretical astronomy from the Naples Observatory.

entries.name: Gottfried Keller
entries.desc: Gottfried Keller was a Swiss poet and writer of German literature. Best known for his novel Green Henry, he became one of the most popular narrators of literary realism in the late 19th century.

entries.name: Florence Nightingale
entries.desc: Founder of modern nursing and a pioneer of statistics in public health.

entries.name: Harriet Tubman
entries.desc: American abolitionist who escaped slavery and led some 70 people to freedom via the Underground Railroad, and later served as a Union spy.

entries.name: Nikolay Danilevsky
entries.desc: Nikolay Yakovlevich Danilevsky was a Russian naturalist, economist, ethnologist, philosopher, historian and ideologue of pan-Slavism and the Slavophile movement.

entries.name: William Thomson, 1st Baron Kelvin
entries.desc: William Thomson, 1st Baron Kelvin was a Scottish mathematician, mathematical physicist and engineer.

entries.name: Dadabhai Naoroji
entries.desc: Indian political leader, founding member of the Indian National Congress, and the first Indian elected to the British Parliament.

entries.name: Henry Dunant
entries.desc: Henry Dunant was a Swiss humanitarian, businessman, social activist, and co-founder of the Red Cross. His humanitarian efforts won him the first Nobel Peace Prize in 1901.

entries.name: Asaph Hall
entries.desc: American astronomer who in 1877 discovered Phobos and Deimos, the two moons of Mars, and determined the orbital mechanics of planetary satellites.

entries.name: Mary Harris Jones
entries.desc: Mary G. Harris Jones, known as Mother Jones from 1897 onward, was an American labor organizer, schoolteacher, and dressmaker who became a prominent union organizer, community organizer, and activist.
entries.tldr: Mary G. Harris Jones, known as Mother Jones from 1897 onward, was an American labor organizer, schoolteacher, and dressmaker who became a prominent union organizer, community organizer, and activist. She helped coordinate major strikes, secure bans on child labor, and co-founded the trade union Industrial Workers of the World (IWW).

entries.name: Leo von Caprivi
entries.desc: Georg Leo Graf von Caprivi de Caprara de Montecuccoli was a German general and statesman. He served as the imperial chancellor of the German Empire from March 1890 to October 1894, succeeding longtime chancellor Otto von Bismarck.

entries.name: Leo von Caprivi
entries.desc: Georg Leo Graf von Caprivi de Caprara de Montecuccoli was a German general and statesman. He served as the imperial chancellor of the German Empire from March 1890 to October 1894, succeeding longtime chancellor Otto von Bismarck.

entries.name: Wilhelm Busch
entries.desc: Heinrich Christian Wilhelm Busch was a German humorist, poet, illustrator, and painter. He published wildly innovative illustrated tales that remain influential to this day.
entries.tldr: Heinrich Christian Wilhelm Busch was a German humorist, poet, illustrator, and painter. He published wildly innovative illustrated tales that remain influential to this day.

entries.name: Wilhelm Busch
entries.desc: Heinrich Christian Wilhelm Busch was a German humorist, poet, illustrator, and painter. He published wildly innovative illustrated tales that remain influential to this day.
entries.tldr: Heinrich Christian Wilhelm Busch was a German humorist, poet, illustrator, and painter. He published wildly innovative illustrated tales that remain influential to this day.

entries.name: Edwin Arnold
entries.desc: English poet and journalist best known for The Light of Asia, a verse biography of the Buddha.

entries.name: Ernesto Teodoro Moneta
entries.desc: Ernesto Teodoro Moneta was an Italian journalist, nationalist, revolutionary soldier and later a pacifist and Nobel Peace Prize Laureate. He adopted the motto In varietate unitas!

entries.name: Élie Ducommun
entries.desc: Élie Ducommun was a Swiss peace activist. He was a Nobel laureate, awarded the 1902 Nobel Peace Prize, which he shared with Charles Albert Gobat.

entries.name: Wilhelm Steinitz
entries.desc: Bohemian-Austrian chess player who became the first official World Chess Champion (1886–1894) and revolutionized chess theory.

entries.name: Henry Campbell-Bannerman
entries.desc: British Liberal Prime Minister 1905–1908 who granted self-government to the former Boer republics and was the first PM officially titled as such.

entries.name: Paul Morphy
entries.desc: American chess prodigy who dominated world chess in the late 1850s, widely regarded as the first unofficial World Chess Champion before retiring from the game.

entries.name: Henry Becque
entries.desc: French dramatist known for naturalistic plays depicting Parisian bourgeois life.

entries.name: Max Bruch
entries.desc: German Romantic composer best known for his Violin Concerto No. 1 in G minor, a cornerstone of the violin repertoire.

entries.name: Liliʻuokalani
entries.desc: Liliʻuokalani was the only queen regnant and the last sovereign monarch of the Hawaiian Kingdom, ruling from January 29, 1891, until the overthrow of the Hawaiian Kingdom on January 17, 1893, in a …
entries.tldr: Liliʻuokalani was the only queen regnant and the last sovereign monarch of the Hawaiian Kingdom, ruling from January 29, 1891, until the overthrow of the Hawaiian Kingdom on January 17, 1893, in a coup that was led by the Committee of Safety, composed of seven foreign residents and six Hawaiian Kingdom subjects of American descent in Honolulu. The composer of "Aloha ʻOe" and numerous other work…

entries.name: John Hay
entries.desc: John Milton Hay was an American statesman and official whose career in government stretched over almost half a century. He served as United States Secretary of State under Presidents William McKinley and Theodore Roosevelt.

entries.name: Victoria, Princess Royal
entries.desc: Victoria, Princess Royal was German Empress and Queen of Prussia as the wife of Frederick III, German Emperor. She was the eldest child of Queen Victoria of the United Kingdom.

entries.name: Berthe Morisot
entries.desc: French Impressionist painter who exhibited at most Impressionist exhibitions and was known for intimate domestic scenes in a distinctive loose style.

entries.name: Emil Theodor Kocher
entries.desc: Swiss surgeon who won the 1909 Nobel Prize in Physiology for pioneering work on thyroid surgery.

entries.name: Georg Ritter von Schönerer
entries.desc: Austrian politician and pan-German nationalist whose virulent antisemitism and rhetoric directly influenced the young Adolf Hitler.

entries.name: Osman Hamdi Bey
entries.desc: Osman Hamdi Bey was an Ottoman Greek administrator, intellectual, art expert and also a prominent and pioneering painter. He was the Ottoman Empire's first archaeologist and first museologist.

entries.name: Friedrich Miescher
entries.desc: Johannes Friedrich Miescher was a Swiss physician and biologist. He was the first scientist to isolate nucleic acid in 1869.

entries.name: Gabriel Fauré
entries.desc: French composer known for his Requiem, piano nocturnes, and chamber music who shaped the transition from Romanticism to modern French music.

entries.name: Alexander III of Russia
entries.desc: Alexander III was Emperor of Russia, King of Poland and Grand Duke of Finland from 13 March 1881 until his death in 1894. He was highly reactionary in domestic affairs.

entries.name: Vasily Surikov
entries.desc: Vasily Ivanovich Surikov was a Russian Realist painter, active in Moscow during Tsars Alexander III and Nicholas II's reigns, renowned for his large history pictures.

entries.name: Vasily Surikov
entries.desc: Vasily Ivanovich Surikov was a Russian Realist painter, active in Moscow during Tsars Alexander III and Nicholas II's reigns, renowned for his large history pictures.

entries.name: Vilfredo Pareto
entries.desc: Italian polymath who formulated the Pareto principle (the 80/20 rule) and made foundational contributions to economics and sociology.

entries.name: Édouard-Henri Avril
entries.desc: Édouard-Henri Avril was a French painter and commercial artist. Under the pseudonym Paul Avril, he was an illustrator of erotic literature.

entries.name: Henry Louis Le Châtelier
entries.desc: Henry Louis Le Chatelier was a French chemist who devised Le Chatelier’s principle, used by chemists to predict the effect a changing condition has on a system in chemical equilibrium.

entries.name: Henri Moissan
entries.desc: French chemist who won the 1906 Nobel Prize in Chemistry for isolating fluorine and pioneering the electric arc furnace.

entries.name: Jacobus Henricus van 't Hoff
entries.desc: Dutch physical chemist who won the inaugural Nobel Prize in Chemistry (1901) for discovering the laws of chemical dynamics and osmotic pressure.

entries.name: José Martí
entries.desc: Cuban poet, journalist, and independence leader who organised the 1895 war against Spanish rule and died in its first skirmish.

entries.name: Paul Sabatier
entries.desc: French chemist who developed a method for hydrogenating organic compounds using metal catalysts, awarded the Nobel Prize in Chemistry in 1912.

entries.name: Ronald Ross
entries.desc: British physician who proved mosquitoes transmit malaria, earning the 1902 Nobel Prize in Physiology or Medicine.

entries.name: Eugen Bleuler
entries.desc: Paul Eugen Bleuler was a Swiss psychiatrist and eugenicist most notable for his influence on modern concepts of mental illness. He coined several psychiatric terms including schizophrenia, schizoid, autism, and depth psychology.

entries.name: Antoni Grabowski
entries.desc: Antoni Grabowski was a Polish chemical engineer, and an activist of the early Esperanto movement. His translations had an influential impact on the development of Esperanto into a language of literature.

entries.name: Christiaan Eijkman
entries.desc: Christiaan Eijkman was a Dutch physician and professor of physiology whose demonstration that beriberi is caused by poor diet led to the discovery of antineuritic vitamins (thiamine). Together with…
entries.tldr: Christiaan Eijkman was a Dutch physician and professor of physiology whose demonstration that beriberi is caused by poor diet led to the discovery of antineuritic vitamins (thiamine). Together with Sir Frederick Hopkins, he received the Nobel Prize for Physiology or Medicine in 1929 for the discovery of vitamins.

entries.name: L. L. Zamenhof
entries.desc: L. L. Zamenhof (1859–1917) was the creator of Esperanto, the most widely used constructed international auxiliary language.

entries.name: Fatma Aliye Topuz
entries.desc: Ottoman Turkish novelist and feminist considered the first female novelist in Turkish literature.

entries.name: Sergei Prokudin-Gorskii
entries.desc: Russian chemist and photographer who pioneered colour photography and documented early 20th-century Russia.

entries.name: Israel Zangwill
entries.desc: British Jewish author and Zionist leader known for novels exploring immigrant life and the play The Melting Pot.

entries.name: Camille Claudel
entries.desc: Camille Rosalie Claudel was a French sculptor known for her figurative works in bronze and marble.

entries.name: Nikolai Marr
entries.desc: Nikolai Yakovlevich Marr was a Georgian-born historian and linguist who gained a reputation as a scholar of the Caucasus.

entries.name: Sofia Okunevska
entries.desc: Sofia Okunevska was a Ukrainian physician, educator, feminist, and scholar. She was the first female Doctor of Medicine and the first female physician in Austria-Hungary.

entries.name: Marie Curie
entries.desc: Physicist and chemist, pioneer of radioactivity and the first person to win two Nobel Prizes.

entries.name: Władysław Reymont
entries.desc: Władysław Stanisław Reymont was a Polish novelist and the laureate of the 1924 Nobel Prize in Literature.

entries.name: Robert Falcon Scott
entries.desc: British Royal Navy officer and Antarctic explorer who led two polar expeditions, perishing on the return from his 1910–13 attempt to reach the South Pole.

entries.name: Henrietta Swan Leavitt
entries.desc: Henrietta Swan Leavitt was an American astronomer. Her discovery of how to effectively measure vast astronomical distances led to a shift in the understanding of the scale and nature of the universe.
entries.tldr: Henrietta Swan Leavitt was an American astronomer. Her discovery of how to effectively measure vast astronomical distances led to a shift in the understanding of the scale and nature of the universe.

entries.name: Henrietta Swan Leavitt
entries.desc: Henrietta Swan Leavitt was an American astronomer. Her discovery of how to effectively measure vast astronomical distances led to a shift in the understanding of the scale and nature of the universe.
entries.tldr: Henrietta Swan Leavitt was an American astronomer. Her discovery of how to effectively measure vast astronomical distances led to a shift in the understanding of the scale and nature of the universe.

entries.name: Mahatma Gandhi
entries.desc: Leader of India's nonviolent independence movement against British rule.

entries.name: Neville Chamberlain
entries.desc: British Prime Minister 1937–1940, defined by his appeasement policy and the Munich Agreement ceding the Sudetenland to Nazi Germany.

entries.name: Johannes Linnankoski
entries.desc: Johannes Linnankoski was a Finnish author and playwright. His most famous work is the romance novel, The Song of the Blood-Red Flower (1905). His primary themes were guilt, punishment, and redemption as moral questions.

entries.name: Johannes Linnankoski
entries.desc: Johannes Linnankoski was a Finnish author and playwright. His most famous work is the romance novel, The Song of the Blood-Red Flower (1905). His primary themes were guilt, punishment, and redemption as moral questions.

entries.name: Victor Grignard
entries.desc: French chemist who discovered the Grignard reagent, an essential tool in organic synthesis, earning the Nobel Prize in Chemistry in 1912.

entries.name: Anton Denikin
entries.desc: Anton Ivanovich Denikin was a Russian military officer who served as a lieutenant general in the Imperial Russian Army and as a leading commander of the anti-Bolshevik White movement during the Rus…
entries.tldr: Anton Ivanovich Denikin was a Russian military officer who served as a lieutenant general in the Imperial Russian Army and as a leading commander of the anti-Bolshevik White movement during the Russian Civil War.

entries.name: Maurice Ravel
entries.desc: Joseph Maurice Ravel was a French composer, pianist and conductor. In the 1920s and 1930s Ravel was internationally regarded as France's greatest living composer.

entries.name: Francis William Aston
entries.desc: British physicist and chemist who won the 1922 Nobel Prize in Chemistry for discovering isotopes in non-radioactive elements via his mass spectrograph.

entries.name: Viktor Dyk
entries.desc: Viktor Dyk was a nationalist Czech poet, prose writer, playwright, politician and political writer. He was sent to jail during the First World War for opposing the Austro-Hungarian empire.
entries.tldr: Viktor Dyk was a nationalist Czech poet, prose writer, playwright, politician and political writer. He was sent to jail during the First World War for opposing the Austro-Hungarian empire. He was one of the signatories of the Manifesto of Czech writers.

entries.name: Viktor Dyk
entries.desc: Viktor Dyk was a nationalist Czech poet, prose writer, playwright, politician and political writer. He was sent to jail during the First World War for opposing the Austro-Hungarian empire.
entries.tldr: Viktor Dyk was a nationalist Czech poet, prose writer, playwright, politician and political writer. He was sent to jail during the First World War for opposing the Austro-Hungarian empire. He was one of the signatories of the Manifesto of Czech writers.

entries.name: Joan Gamper
entries.desc: Hans Max Gamper-Haessig, commonly known as Joan Gamper, was a Swiss-born football executive and versatile athlete. He founded football clubs in Switzerland and Spain, most notably Barcelona.

entries.name: Gustav Stresemann
entries.desc: German statesman who as chancellor and foreign minister steered Weimar Germany toward international reconciliation, sharing the 1926 Nobel Peace Prize.

entries.name: Pyotr Wrangel
entries.desc: Baron Pyotr Nikolayevich Wrangel, nicknamed the "Black Baron", was a Russian military officer who served as a commanding general in the anti-Bolshevik Volunteer Army during the Russian Civil War. I…
entries.tldr: Baron Pyotr Nikolayevich Wrangel, nicknamed the "Black Baron", was a Russian military officer who served as a commanding general in the anti-Bolshevik Volunteer Army during the Russian Civil War. In 1920, he became the last commander-in-chief of the White forces in Southern Russia, which he reorganized as the Russian Army.

entries.name: Franz Reichelt
entries.desc: Austro-Hungarian tailor and inventor known as “the Flying Tailor” who died testing his parachute suit by jumping from the Eiffel Tower.

entries.name: Egon Friedell
entries.desc: Austrian cultural historian, playwright, and actor whose three-volume Cultural History of the Modern Age remains a landmark of popular historiography.

entries.name: Marcel Grossmann
entries.desc: Marcel Grossmann was a Swiss mathematician who was a friend and classmate of Albert Einstein.

entries.name: Otto Bauer
entries.desc: Otto Bauer was an Austrian politician who was one of the founders and leading thinkers of the Austromarxists. He served as Foreign Minister of the Republic of German-Austria.

entries.name: Lili Elbe
entries.desc: Danish painter and one of the earliest known recipients of gender reassignment surgery.

entries.name: Inayat Khan
entries.desc: Inayat Khan Rehmat Khan was an Indian professor of musicology, singer, poet, philosopher, writer, and pioneer of the transmission of Sufism to the West.

entries.name: Douglas Fairbanks
entries.desc: American actor famed for swashbuckling silent-film roles including Zorro and Robin Hood, and co-founder of United Artists.

entries.name: Amedeo Modigliani
entries.desc: Amedeo Clemente Modigliani was an Italian painter and sculptor of the École de Paris who worked mainly in France. He is known for portraits and nudes in a modern style.

entries.name: Hermann Rorschach
entries.desc: Hermann Rorschach was a Swiss psychiatrist and psychoanalyst who developed the famous inkblot test that bears his name.

entries.name: Jacques Feyder
entries.desc: Jacques Feyder was a Belgian film director, screenwriter and actor who worked principally in France. He was associated with the style of poetic realism in French cinema.

entries.name: Engelbert Dollfuss
entries.desc: Engelbert Dollfuss was an Austrian politician and dictator who served as chancellor of Austria between 1932 and 1934.

entries.name: Joachim von Ribbentrop
entries.desc: German politician and diplomat who served as Nazi Germany's Foreign Minister 1938–1945; executed at Nuremberg as a war criminal.

entries.name: Vicente Huidobro
entries.desc: Vicente García-Huidobro Fernández, known by the pseudonym Vicente Huidobro, was a Chilean poet, writer, editor and literary critic. He was the founder of Creationism.

entries.name: Grand Duchess Olga Nikolaevna of Russia
entries.desc: Eldest daughter of Russia's last tsar Nicholas II, executed with her family by Bolsheviks in 1918.

entries.name: Italo Balbo
entries.desc: Italian fascist politician and aviator who led pioneering transatlantic air expeditions in the 1930s and served as Governor-General of Libya until his death.

entries.name: Glenn Miller
entries.desc: American big band conductor and trombonist whose Orchestra was one of the most popular ensembles of the Swing era before he disappeared over the English Channel in 1944.

entries.name: Jean Harlow
entries.desc: American actress and 1930s sex symbol dubbed the ‘Platinum Blonde’ whose comedic roles at MGM made her one of Hollywood’s biggest stars before her early death at 26.

entries.name: Manshuk Mametova
entries.desc: Kazakh machine-gunner in the Red Army who single-handedly held off a German assault near Nevel in 1943, posthumously awarded Hero of the Soviet Union.

entries.name: Eugène François Vidocq
entries.desc: Eugène-François Vidocq was a French criminal turned criminalist. He was the founder and first director of France's Sûreté Nationale, and the head of the first known private detective agency.

entries.name: Alexander Emanuel Agassiz
entries.desc: Alexander Emmanuel Rodolphe Agassiz was an American scientist and engineer. He was the son of Louis Agassiz and stepson of Elizabeth Cabot Agassiz.

entries.name: Louis Agassiz
entries.desc: Jean Louis Rodolphe Agassiz was a Swiss-born American biologist and geologist who is recognized as a scholar of Earth's natural history.

entries.name: Johann Jakob Balmer
entries.desc: Johann Jakob Balmer was a Swiss mathematician best known for his work in physics, the Balmer series of the hydrogen atom.

entries.name: Pauline Viardot
entries.desc: Pauline Viardot was a French dramatic mezzo-soprano, composer and pedagogue of Spanish descent.

entries.name: Johann Heinrich Lambert
entries.desc: Johann Heinrich Lambert was a polymath who made important contributions to mathematics, physics, philosophy, astronomy and map projections.

entries.name: Alfred Werner
entries.desc: Alfred Werner was a Swiss chemist who won the Nobel Prize in Chemistry in 1913 for proposing the octahedral configuration of transition metal complexes.

entries.name: Charles Édouard Guillaume
entries.desc: Charles-Édouard Guillaume was a Swiss physicist who received the Nobel Prize in Physics in 1920 for his discovery of anomalies in nickel steel alloys.

entries.name: Huldrych Zwingli
entries.desc: Huldrych Zwingli was a Swiss Christian theologian, musician, and leader of the Reformation in Switzerland.

entries.name: Germaine de Staël
entries.desc: Anne Louise Germaine de Staël-Holstein, commonly known as Madame de Staël, was a prominent French novelist, woman of letters, philosopher, and political theorist.

entries.name: Go-Komatsu
entries.desc: Emperor Go-Komatsu was the 100th emperor of Japan, according to the traditional order of succession, and the sixth and final Emperor of the Northern Court.

entries.name: Johanna Spyri
entries.desc: Johanna Spyri was a Swiss author of novels, notably children's stories. She wrote the popular book Heidi.

entries.name: Augustin-Pyramus de Candolle
entries.desc: Augustin Pyramus de Candolle was a Swiss botanist who documented hundreds of plant families and created a new natural plant classification system.

entries.name: Millard Fillmore
entries.desc: Millard Fillmore was the 13th president of the United States, serving from 1850 to 1853. He was the last president to be a member of the Whig Party while in the White House.

entries.name: Jacques Necker
entries.desc: Jacques Necker was a Genevan banker, financier and statesman who served as finance minister of France for Louis XVI.

entries.name: Arnold Böcklin
entries.desc: Arnold Böcklin was a Swiss Symbolist painter. His five versions of the Isle of the Dead inspired works by several late Romantic composers.

entries.name: Ismail II
entries.desc: Ismail II was the third shah of Safavid Iran from 1576 to 1577.

entries.name: Benjamin Constant
entries.desc: Henri-Benjamin Constant de Rebecque, or simply Benjamin Constant, was a Swiss and French political thinker, activist and writer on political theory and religion.

entries.name: Angelica Kauffmann
entries.desc: Maria Anna Angelika Kauffmann was a Swiss painter who had a successful career in London and Rome. She was one of two female painters among the founding members of the Royal Academy of Art in London in 1768.

entries.name: Franklin Pierce
entries.desc: Franklin Pierce was the 14th president of the United States, serving from 1853 to 1857. He signed the Kansas–Nebraska Act and enforced the Fugitive Slave Act.

entries.name: Ammonius Hermiae
entries.desc: Ammonius Hermiae was a Greek Neoplatonist philosopher from Alexandria, son of Hermias and Aedesia. He studied under Proclus in Athens and held a public chair in Alexandria from the 470s.
entries.tldr: Ammonius Hermiae was a Greek Neoplatonist philosopher from Alexandria, Byzantine Egypt, son of the philosophers Hermias and Aedesia and grandson of Syrianus. He studied under Proclus in Roman Athens before returning to Alexandria, where he held a public chair from the 470s and taught for most of his life. His commentaries on Aristotle were highly influential in later Neoplatonist tradition.

entries.name: Francesco Borromini
entries.desc: Italian Baroque architect from Swiss Ticino who shaped Roman architecture alongside Bernini and Pietro da Cortona.
entries.tldr: Francesco Borromini, born Francesco Castelli in the Swiss canton of Ticino, was a leading figure in Roman Baroque architecture alongside Gian Lorenzo Bernini and Pietro da Cortona. His bold use of geometry and dynamic forms broke with Renaissance convention, producing masterpieces such as San Carlo alle Quattro Fontane and Sant'Ivo alla Sapienza in Rome.

entries.name: Albrecht von Haller
entries.desc: Swiss anatomist, physiologist, naturalist, and poet sometimes called “the father of modern physiology.”
entries.tldr: Albrecht von Haller was a Swiss anatomist, physiologist, naturalist, encyclopedist, bibliographer and poet, sometimes called “the father of modern physiology.” A pupil of Herman Boerhaave and Jacob Winslow, he made foundational contributions to understanding muscle irritability and nervous sensation. He also wrote celebrated poetry and compiled massive bibliographies of medical and botanical literature.

entries.name: Guillaume Henri Dufour
entries.desc: Swiss general, structural engineer, and topographer who led the Confederation to victory in 1847 and chaired the First Geneva Convention.
entries.tldr: Guillaume Henri Dufour was a Swiss military officer, structural engineer, and topographer who served under Napoleon I. He held the Swiss office of General four times, most notably leading Confederation forces to victory against the Sonderbund in 1847. In 1864 he presided over the First Geneva Convention, which established the International Red Cross.

entries.name: James Buchanan
entries.desc: 15th president of the United States (1857–1861), the only president never to marry.
entries.tldr: James Buchanan Jr. was the 15th president of the United States, serving from 1857 to 1861. He also served as the 17th United States Secretary of State (1845–1849) and represented Pennsylvania in both houses of Congress. His presidency is largely remembered for failing to prevent the secession of Southern states on the eve of the Civil War.

entries.name: Conrad Ferdinand Meyer
entries.desc: Swiss poet and historical novelist, a master of literary realism best known for narrative ballads like “Die Füße im Feuer.”
entries.tldr: Conrad Ferdinand Meyer was a Swiss poet and historical novelist, a master of literary realism who is mainly remembered for stirring narrative ballads like “Die Füße im Feuer.” His novellas, set in the Italian Renaissance and Swiss history, combine psychological depth with precise, restrained prose.

entries.name: Annemarie Schwarzenbach
entries.desc: Swiss writer, journalist, and photographer whose anti-fascist activism drove her into exile and whose restless travels shaped her literary output.
entries.tldr: Annemarie Schwarzenbach was a Swiss writer, journalist, and photographer whose androgynous image suited bohemian Berlin society of the 1930s. Her anti-fascist campaigning forced her into exile, where she became close to the family of Thomas Mann. Her travel writing and novels reflect a life of perpetual movement across Europe, Asia, and the Americas.

entries.name: Isabelle de Charrière
entries.desc: Dutch-Swiss Enlightenment writer and composer known for letters, novels, pamphlets, and music composed in Colombier, Neuchâtel.
entries.tldr: Isabelle de Charrière, also known as Belle van Zuylen, was a Dutch and Swiss writer of the Enlightenment who spent the latter half of her life in Colombier, Neuchâtel. She is best known for her letters and novels, but also wrote pamphlets, composed music, and authored plays. She engaged keenly with the society and politics of her time and corresponded with notable contemporaries including James Boswell and Benjamin Constant.

entries.name: Jules de Goncourt
entries.desc: French writer who co-authored novels and art criticism with his brother Edmond; died at 39 in Paris.
entries.tldr: Jules Alfred Huot de Goncourt was a French writer who published books together with his brother Edmond, including novels, art criticism, and journals of Parisian literary life. Born and dying in Paris, Jules died at the age of 39 from a stroke brought on by syphilis. The Goncourt Academy and its literary prize were established in his and his brother's honor.

entries.name: Heinrich Wölfflin
entries.desc: Swiss art historian whose formal principles of style — linear vs. painterly, plane vs. recession — shaped 20th-century art history.
entries.tldr: Heinrich Wölfflin was a Swiss art historian, aesthetician, and educator whose objective classifying principles were influential in the development of formal analysis in art history in the early 20th century. He taught at Basel, Berlin, and Munich and formulated paired concepts (e.g. linear vs. painterly, closed vs. open form) that became foundational tools in stylistic analysis.

entries.name: Charles Sturm
entries.desc: French mathematician known for Sturm’s theorem, which counts the real roots of a polynomial within an interval.
entries.tldr: Jacques Charles François Sturm was a French mathematician who made a significant contribution to equation theory with Sturm’s theorem, an algorithm for counting the distinct real roots of a polynomial within a given interval. He also contributed to differential equations and mathematical physics.

entries.name: Jakob Steiner
entries.desc: Swiss mathematician who revolutionized synthetic geometry, developing projective methods without the use of coordinates.
entries.tldr: Jakob Steiner was a Swiss mathematician who worked primarily in geometry, revolutionizing synthetic geometry by developing projective methods without coordinates. His work on conic sections, algebraic curves, and inversive geometry earned him a professorship at Berlin and lasting influence in pure mathematics.

entries.name: Albert von Kölliker
entries.desc: Swiss anatomist, physiologist, and histologist who co-founded modern histology and established that nerve fibers are extensions of nerve cells.
entries.tldr: Albert von Kölliker was a Swiss anatomist, physiologist, and histologist who co-founded modern histology and established that nerve fibers are extensions of nerve cells. He taught at Würzburg for decades and authored influential textbooks on microscopic anatomy that shaped the discipline through the 19th century.

entries.name: Charles Bonnet
entries.desc: Genevan naturalist and philosophical writer who coined the term phyllotaxis and was among the first to document parthenogenesis in aphids.
entries.tldr: Charles Bonnet was a Genevan naturalist and philosophical writer who coined the term phyllotaxis to describe the arrangement of leaves on a plant. He was among the first to observe parthenogenetic reproduction in aphids and established that insects respired through their spiracles. He also wrote influential works on philosophy of nature and soul.

entries.name: Gaspard Bauhin
entries.desc: Swiss botanist whose Pinax theatri botanici (1623) classified thousands of plants, anticipating Linnaeus’s binomial nomenclature.
entries.tldr: Gaspard Bauhin was a Swiss botanist whose Pinax theatri botanici (1623) described thousands of plants and classified them in a manner that draws comparisons to the later binomial nomenclature of Linnaeus. A disciple of the Italian physician Girolamo Mercuriale, he also contributed to human anatomical nomenclature and taught medicine at Basel.

entries.name: Charles Ferdinand Ramuz
entries.desc: French-speaking Swiss novelist and poet whose works depict rural Vaud peasant life with austere, rhythmic prose.
entries.tldr: Charles Ferdinand Ramuz was a French-speaking Swiss writer whose novels and poems drew on the landscape and peasant life of the Vaud region. His austere, rhythmic prose and focus on collective experience set him apart from Parisian literary trends. He collaborated with Igor Stravinsky on L’Histoire du soldat (1918).

entries.name: Jean-André Deluc
entries.desc: Geologist, natural philosopher, and meteorologist from Geneva who coined the term ‘geology’ and invented precision instruments for measuring temperature and pressure.
entries.tldr: Jean-André Deluc was a geologist, natural philosopher, and meteorologist from the Republic of Geneva who is credited with coining the term ‘geology.’ He devised precision measuring instruments and made important contributions to meteorology and the theory of Earth’s history. He later served as reader to Queen Charlotte of Great Britain.

entries.name: Jean-Étienne Liotard
entries.desc: Genevan painter and pastellist renowned for strikingly naturalistic portraits and Orientalist scenes painted across Europe and Istanbul.
entries.tldr: Jean-Étienne Liotard was a Genevan painter, pastellist, printmaker, art theorist, and art dealer born to exiled French Huguenots. He spent his career in Rome, Istanbul, Paris, Vienna, London, and Amsterdam, and is best known for his detailed, naturalistic portraits in pastel and Orientalist scenes of daily life. His Turkish dress and long beard made him one of the most recognizable artists of the 18th century.

entries.name: Salomon Gessner
entries.desc: Swiss painter, graphic artist, and poet best known for his Idylls; co-founder of the Helvetic Society and first publisher of the Neue Zürcher Zeitung.
entries.tldr: Salomon Gessner was a Swiss painter, graphic artist, government official, newspaper publisher, and poet, best known for his Idylls. He was a co-founder of the Helvetic Society and the first publisher and editor of the Neue Zürcher Zeitung. His pastoral poetry was widely translated across Europe and influenced the Romantic movement.

entries.name: Alphonse Pyramus de Candolle
entries.desc: French-Swiss botanist who continued his father’s Prodromus and pioneered phytogeography and the study of plant origins.
entries.tldr: Alphonse Louis Pierre Pyramus de Candolle was a French-Swiss botanist, son of the Swiss botanist Augustin Pyramus de Candolle. He continued his father’s monumental Prodromus Systematis Naturalis Regni Vegetabilis and made significant contributions to phytogeography and the scientific study of plant origins and distribution.

entries.name: George Gershwin
entries.desc: George Gershwin was an American composer and pianist whose compositions spanned jazz, popular and classical music. Among his best-known works are the songs "Swanee" (1919) and "Fascinating Rhythm" …
entries.tldr: George Gershwin was an American composer and pianist whose compositions spanned jazz, popular and classical music. Among his best-known works are the songs "Swanee" (1919) and "Fascinating Rhythm" (1924), the orchestral compositions Rhapsody in Blue (1924) and An American in Paris (1928), the jazz standards "Embraceable You" (1928) and "I Got Rhythm" (1930) and the opera Porgy and Bess (1935), …

entries.name: Heinrich Bullinger
entries.desc: Heinrich Bullinger was a Swiss Reformer and theologian, the successor of Huldrych Zwingli as head of the Church of Zürich and a pastor at the Grossmünster. One of the most important leaders of the …
entries.tldr: Heinrich Bullinger was a Swiss Reformer and theologian, the successor of Huldrych Zwingli as head of the Church of Zürich and a pastor at the Grossmünster.
