One of the most important leaders of the Swiss Reformation, Bullinger co-authored the Helvetic Confessions and collaborated with John Calvin to work out a Reformed doctrine of the Lord's Supper.

entries.name: Johann Jakob Bachofen
entries.desc: Johann Jakob Bachofen was a Swiss antiquarian, jurist, philologist, anthropologist, and professor of Roman law at the University of Basel from 1841 to 1844.
entries.tldr: Johann Jakob Bachofen was a Swiss antiquarian, jurist, philologist, anthropologist, and professor of Roman law at the University of Basel from 1841 to 1844.

entries.name: Marquis de Sade
entries.desc: Donatien Alphonse François, Marquis de Sade was a French writer, libertine, political activist, and nobleman best known for his libertine novels and imprisonment for sex crimes, blasphemy, and porn…
entries.tldr: Donatien Alphonse François, Marquis de Sade was a French writer, libertine, political activist, and nobleman best known for his libertine novels and imprisonment for sex crimes, blasphemy, and pornography. His works include novels, short stories, plays, dialogues, and political tracts. Some of these were published under his own name during his lifetime, but most appeared anonymously or posthumo…

entries.name: Carl Nägeli
entries.desc: Carl Wilhelm von Nägeli was a Swiss botanist. He studied cell division and pollination but became known as the man who discouraged Gregor Mendel from further work on genetics.
entries.tldr: Carl Wilhelm von Nägeli was a Swiss botanist. He studied cell division and pollination but became known as the man who discouraged Gregor Mendel from further work on genetics. He rejected natural selection as a mechanism of evolution, favouring orthogenesis, though the term was only coined in 1893, two years after his death, driven by a supposed "inner perfecting principle".

entries.name: Henri-Frédéric Amiel
entries.desc: Henri Frédéric Amiel was a Swiss moral philosopher, poet, and critic.
entries.tldr: Henri Frédéric Amiel was a Swiss moral philosopher, poet, and critic.

entries.name: Johann Kaspar Lavater
entries.desc: Johann Kaspar Lavater was a Swiss poet, writer, philosopher, physiognomist and Reformed theologian.
entries.tldr: Johann Kaspar Lavater was a Swiss poet, writer, philosopher, physiognomist and Reformed theologian.

entries.name: Alexandre Yersin
entries.desc: Alexandre Émile John Yersin was a Swiss-French physician and bacteriologist. He is remembered for his work as a pioneer in microbiology and immunology.
entries.tldr: Alexandre Émile John Yersin was a Swiss-French physician and bacteriologist. He is remembered for his work as a pioneer in microbiology and immunology. Yersin is the co-discoverer of both the Diphtheria and Tetanus toxins and of the bacillus responsible for the bubonic plague.

entries.name: Jakob Friedrich Ehrhart
entries.desc: Jakob Friedrich Ehrhart was a German botanist, a pupil of Carl Linnaeus at Uppsala University, and later director of the Botanical Garden of Hannover, where he produced several major botanical work…
entries.tldr: Jakob Friedrich Ehrhart was a German botanist, a pupil of Carl Linnaeus at Uppsala University, and later director of the Botanical Garden of Hannover, where he produced several major botanical works between 1780 and 1793. Ehrhart was the first author to use the rank of subspecies in botanical literature, and he published many subspecific names between 1780 and 1789. Ehrhart issued several exsic…

entries.name: Nicolaus II Bernoulli
entries.desc: Nicolaus II Bernoulli was a Swiss mathematician as were his father Johann Bernoulli and one of his brothers, Daniel Bernoulli. He was one of the many prominent mathematicians in the Bernoulli family.
entries.tldr: Nicolaus II Bernoulli was a Swiss mathematician as were his father Johann Bernoulli and one of his brothers, Daniel Bernoulli. He was one of the many prominent mathematicians in the Bernoulli family.

entries.name: Jost Bürgi
entries.desc: Jost Bürgi, active primarily at the courts in Kassel and Prague, was a Swiss clockmaker, mathematician, and writer. Burgi was the brother-in-law and adoptive father of Benjamin Bramer.
entries.tldr: Jost Bürgi, active primarily at the courts in Kassel and Prague, was a Swiss clockmaker, mathematician, and writer. Burgi was the brother-in-law and adoptive father of Benjamin Bramer.

entries.name: Jean Charles Léonard de Sismondi
entries.desc: Jean Charles Léonard de Sismondi, also known as Jean Charles Leonard Simonde de Sismondi, whose real surname was Simonde, was a Swiss historian and political economist, who is best known for his wo…
entries.tldr: Jean Charles Léonard de Sismondi, also known as Jean Charles Leonard Simonde de Sismondi, whose real surname was Simonde, was a Swiss historian and political economist, who is best known for his works on French and Italian history, and his economic ideas. His Nouveaux principes d'économie politique, ou de la richesse dans ses rapports avec la population (1819) represents the first liberal criti…

entries.name: Niels Henrik Abel
entries.desc: Niels Henrik Abel was a Norwegian mathematician who made pioneering contributions in a variety of fields. His most famous single result is the first complete proof demonstrating the impossibility o…
entries.tldr: Niels Henrik Abel was a Norwegian mathematician who made pioneering contributions in a variety of fields. His most famous single result is the first complete proof demonstrating the impossibility of solving the general quintic equation in radicals. This question was one of the outstanding open problems of his day, and had been unresolved for over 250 years.

entries.name: Hasan-i Sabbah
entries.desc: Hasan-i Sabbah, also known as Hasan I of Alamut, was a military leader, founder of the Nizari Ismai'li sect, Order of Assassins, as well as the Nizari Ismaili state, ruling from 1090 to 1124 AD.
entries.tldr: Hasan-i Sabbah, also known as Hasan I of Alamut, was a military leader, founder of the Nizari Ismai'li sect, Order of Assassins, as well as the Nizari Ismaili state, ruling from 1090 to 1124 AD.

entries.name: Pius VII
entries.desc: Pope Pius VII was head of the Catholic Church from 14 March 1800 to his death in August 1823. He was the leader of the Papal States from June 1800 to 17 May 1809 and again from 1814 to his death.
entries.tldr: Pope Pius VII was head of the Catholic Church from 14 March 1800 to his death in August 1823. He was the leader of the Papal States from June 1800 to 17 May 1809 and again from 1814 to his death. Chiaramonti was also a monk of the Order of Saint Benedict in addition to being a well-known theologian and bishop.

entries.name: Pietro Locatelli
entries.desc: Pietro Antonio Locatelli was an Italian Baroque composer and violinist.
entries.tldr: Pietro Antonio Locatelli was an Italian Baroque composer and violinist.

entries.name: Jean-Louis Guez de Balzac
entries.desc: Jean-Louis Guez de Balzac was a French author in Baroque Précieuses style, best known for his epistolary essays, which were widely circulated and read in his day. He was one of the founding members…
entries.tldr: Jean-Louis Guez de Balzac was a French author in Baroque Précieuses style, best known for his epistolary essays, which were widely circulated and read in his day. He was one of the founding members of the Académie française.

entries.name: Thomas Fuller
entries.desc: Thomas Fuller was an English churchman and historian. He is now remembered for his writings, particularly his Worthies of England, published in 1662, after his death.
entries.tldr: Thomas Fuller was an English churchman and historian. He is now remembered for his writings, particularly his Worthies of England, published in 1662, after his death. He was a prolific author, and one of the first English writers able to live by his pen.

entries.name: Edmond de Goncourt
entries.desc: Edmond Louis Antoine Huot de Goncourt was a French writer, literary critic, art critic, book publisher and the founder of the Académie Goncourt.
entries.tldr: Edmond Louis Antoine Huot de Goncourt was a French writer, literary critic, art critic, book publisher and the founder of the Académie Goncourt.

entries.name: Frederick the Fair
entries.desc: Frederick the Fair or the Handsome, from the House of Habsburg, was the duke of Austria and Styria from 1308 as well as the anti-king of Germany from 1314 until 1325 and then co-king until his death.
entries.tldr: Frederick the Fair or the Handsome, from the House of Habsburg, was the duke of Austria and Styria from 1308 as well as the anti-king of Germany from 1314 until 1325 and then co-king until his death.

entries.name: Richard Francis Burton
entries.desc: Sir Richard Francis Burton was a British explorer, army officer, writer and scholar. He was famed for his travels and explorations in Asia, Africa and South America, as well as his extensive knowle…
entries.tldr: Sir Richard Francis Burton was a British explorer, army officer, writer and scholar. He was famed for his travels and explorations in Asia, Africa and South America, as well as his extensive knowledge of languages and cultures, speaking up to 29 different languages.

entries.name: John Hanning Speke
entries.desc: John Hanning Speke was an English explorer and army officer who made three exploratory expeditions to Africa. He is most associated with the search for the source of the Nile and, with Richard Burt…
entries.tldr: John Hanning Speke was an English explorer and army officer who made three exploratory expeditions to Africa. He is most associated with the search for the source of the Nile and, with Richard Burton, was the first European to reach Lake Victoria.

entries.name: Şehzade Mustafa
entries.desc:
Şehzade Mustafa may refer to:Şehzade Mustafa (1450–1474), Ottoman prince, son of Mehmed II and Gülşah Hatun
Şehzade Mustafa, Ottoman prince, son of Suleiman the Magnificent and Mahidevran Hatun
entries.tldr:
Şehzade Mustafa may refer to:Şehzade Mustafa (1450–1474), Ottoman prince, son of Mehmed II and Gülşah Hatun

entries.name: William James
entries.desc: William James was an American philosopher and psychologist. The first educator to offer a psychology course in the United States, he is considered to be one of the leading thinkers of the late 19th…
entries.tldr: William James was an American philosopher and psychologist. The first educator to offer a psychology course in the United States, he is considered to be one of the leading thinkers of the late 19th century, one of the most influential philosophers and is often dubbed the "father of American psychology".

entries.name: Pompey
entries.desc: Gnaeus Pompeius Magnus, known in English as Pompey or Pompey the Great, was a Roman general and statesman who was prominent in the final decades of the Roman Republic. As a young man, he was a part…
entries.tldr: Gnaeus Pompeius Magnus, known in English as Pompey or Pompey the Great, was a Roman general and statesman who was prominent in the final decades of the Roman Republic. As a young man, he was a partisan and protégé of the dictator Sulla, after whose death he achieved significant military and political success.

entries.name: Henry Draper
entries.desc: Henry Draper was an American medical doctor and amateur astronomer. He is best known today as a pioneer of astrophotography.
entries.tldr: Henry Draper was an American medical doctor and amateur astronomer. He is best known today as a pioneer of astrophotography.

entries.name: William III of the Netherlands
entries.desc: William III was King of the Netherlands and Grand Duke of Luxembourg from 1849 until his death in 1890, and was also the Duke of Limburg from 1849 until the abolition of the duchy in 1866. Having r…
entries.tldr: William III was King of the Netherlands and Grand Duke of Luxembourg from 1849 until his death in 1890, and was also the Duke of Limburg from 1849 until the abolition of the duchy in 1866. Having reigned for 41 years, he is the second-longest reigning Dutch monarch, only surpassed by his daughter, Wilhelmina.

entries.name: Methodios I of Constantinople
entries.desc: Methodius I of Constantinople or Methodios I was Ecumenical Patriarch of Constantinople from 11 March 843 to 14 June 847. He was born in Syracuse and died in Constantinople.
entries.tldr: Methodius I of Constantinople or Methodios I was Ecumenical Patriarch of Constantinople from 11 March 843 to 14 June 847. He was born in Syracuse and died in Constantinople. His feast day is celebrated on June 14 in both the East and the West.

entries.name: Petko Slaveykov
entries.desc: Petko Rachov Slaveykov was a Bulgarian poet, publicist, politician and folklorist.
entries.tldr: Petko Rachov Slaveykov was a Bulgarian poet, publicist, politician and folklorist.

entries.name: Sasan
entries.desc: Sasan, considered the eponymous ancestor of the Sasanian Dynasty in Persia, was "a great warrior and hunter" and a Zoroastrian high priest in Pars. He lived sometime near the fall of the Arsacid (P…
entries.tldr: Sasan, considered the eponymous ancestor of the Sasanian Dynasty in Persia, was "a great warrior and hunter" and a Zoroastrian high priest in Pars. He lived sometime near the fall of the Arsacid (Parthian) Empire in the early 3rd century.

entries.name: Nebuchadnezzar II
entries.desc: Nebuchadnezzar II, more correctly spelled Nebuchadrezzar II, meaning "Nabu, watch over my heir", was the second king of the Neo-Babylonian Empire, ruling from the death of his father Nabopolassar i…
entries.tldr: Nebuchadnezzar II, more correctly spelled Nebuchadrezzar II, meaning "Nabu, watch over my heir", was the second king of the Neo-Babylonian Empire, ruling from the death of his father Nabopolassar in 605 BC to his own death in 562 BC. Often titled Nebuchadnezzar the Great, he is regarded as the empire's greatest king, famous for his military campaigns in the Levant and their role in Jewish histo…

entries.name: Nebuchadnezzar I
entries.desc: Nebuchadnezzar I was the fourth king of the Second Dynasty of Isin and Fourth Dynasty of Babylon. He ruled for 22 years according to the Babylonian King List C, and was the most prominent monarch o…
entries.tldr: Nebuchadnezzar I was the fourth king of the Second Dynasty of Isin and Fourth Dynasty of Babylon. He ruled for 22 years according to the Babylonian King List C, and was the most prominent monarch of this dynasty. He is best known for his victory over Elam and the recovery of the cultic idol of Marduk.

entries.name: James II of England
entries.desc: James II and VII was King of England and Ireland as James II and King of Scotland as James VII from February 1685 until he was deposed in the 1688 Glorious Revolution. The last Catholic monarch of …
entries.tldr: James II and VII was King of England and Ireland as James II and King of Scotland as James VII from February 1685 until he was deposed in the 1688 Glorious Revolution. The last Catholic monarch of England, Scotland, and Ireland, his reign was marked by conflicts over religion, absolutism and the divine right of kings; his deposition ended a century of political and civil strife by confirming th…

entries.name: Jurji Zaydan
entries.desc: Jurji Zaydan was a prolific Lebanese novelist, journalist, editor and teacher, most noted for his creation of the magazine Al-Hilal, which he used to serialize his twenty three historical novels.
entries.tldr: Jurji Zaydan was a prolific Lebanese novelist, journalist, editor and teacher, most noted for his creation of the magazine Al-Hilal, which he used to serialize his twenty three historical novels.

entries.name: Thomas Paine
entries.desc: Thomas Paine was an English-born American Founding Father, inventor, political philosopher, and statesman. His pamphlets Common Sense (1776) and The American Crisis (1776–1783) framed the Patriot a…

He excelled in numerous fields including but not limited to mathematics, astronomy, cartography, geography, metallurgy, mechanical engineering, hydraulic engineering, poetry, and statesmanship.

entries.name: Wilhelm Ostwald
entries.desc: Wilhelm Friedrich Ostwald was a Baltic German chemist and philosopher. Ostwald is credited with being one of the founders of the field of physical chemistry, with Jacobus Henricus van 't Hoff, Walt…
entries.tldr: Wilhelm Friedrich Ostwald was a Baltic German chemist and philosopher. Ostwald is credited with being one of the founders of the field of physical chemistry, with Jacobus Henricus van 't Hoff, Walther Nernst and Svante Arrhenius. He received the Nobel Prize in Chemistry in 1909 for his scientific contributions to the fields of catalysis, chemical equilibria and reaction velocities.

entries.name: William Hazlitt
entries.desc: William Hazlitt was an English essayist, drama and literary critic, painter, social commentator, and philosopher. He is now considered one of the greatest critics and essayists in the history of th…
entries.tldr: William Hazlitt was an English essayist, drama and literary critic, painter, social commentator, and philosopher. He is now considered one of the greatest critics and essayists in the history of the English language, placed in the company of Samuel Johnson and George Orwell. He is also acknowledged as the finest art critic of his age.

entries.name: Ziryab

entries.tldr: Abu al-Hasan ‘Ali Ibn Nafi’, commonly known as Ziryab, was a singer, oud and lute player, composer, poet, and teacher. He lived and worked in what is now Iraq, West Asia, and Andalusia during the medieval Islamic period. He was also a polymath, with knowledge in astronomy, geography, meteorology, botanics, cosmetics, culinary art, and fashion.

entries.name: Benedict XIV
entries.desc: Pope Benedict XIV, born Prospero Lorenzo Lambertini, was head of the Catholic Church and ruler of the Papal States from 17 August 1740 to his death on 3 May 1758.

entries.tldr: Frédéric François Chopin was a Polish composer and virtuoso pianist of the early Romantic period who wrote primarily for solo piano. He has maintained worldwide renown as a leading composer of his era whose “poetic genius was based on a professional technique that was without equal in his generation”.

He is described as “the Father of French Indochina”, and was seen as one of the most active and effective governors general of Indochina.

entries.tldr: Nitobe Inazō  was a Japanese agronomist, diplomat, political scientist, politician, and writer. He studied at Sapporo Agricultural College under the influence of its first president William S. Clark and later went to the United States to study agricultural policy.

entries.name: Maxim Gorky
entries.desc: Alexei Maximovich Peshkov, popularly known as Maxim Gorky, was a Russian and Soviet writer, journalist, and proponent of socialism. He was nominated five times for the Nobel Prize in Literature.
entries.tldr: Alexei Maximovich Peshkov, popularly known as Maxim Gorky, was a Russian and Soviet writer, journalist, and proponent of socialism. He was nominated five times for the Nobel Prize in Literature. Before his success as an author, he travelled widely across the Russian Empire, changing jobs frequently; these experiences would later influence his writing.

One of the most prolific artists active in the last decades of the Ancien Régime, Fragonard produced more than 550 paintings, of which only five are dated. entries.name: Auguste Comte
entries.desc: Isidore Auguste Marie François Xavier Comte was a French philosopher, mathematician and writer who formulated the doctrine of positivism. He is often regarded as the first philosopher of science in…
entries.tldr: Isidore Auguste Marie François Xavier Comte was a French philosopher, mathematician and writer who formulated the doctrine of positivism. He is often regarded as the first philosopher of science in the modern sense of the term. Comte’s ideas were fundamental to the development of sociology, with him inventing the very term and treating the discipline as the crowning achievement of the sciences.

In 1907, he received the Nobel Prize in Physics, becoming the first American to win the Nobel Prize in a science. He was the founder and the first head of the physics departments of the Case School of Applied Science and the Unive…

entries.name: Alexander VII
entries.desc: Pope Alexander VII, born Fabio Chigi, was head of the Catholic Church and ruler of the Papal States from 7 April 1655 to his death, in May 1667.
entries.tldr: Pope Alexander VII, born Fabio Chigi, was head of the Catholic Church and ruler of the Papal States from 7 April 1655 to his death, in May 1667.

entries.name: George III of Great Britain
entries.desc: George III was King of Great Britain and Ireland from 25 October 1760 until his death in 1820. The Acts of Union 1800 unified Great Britain and Ireland into the United Kingdom of Great Britain and …
entries.tldr: George III was King of Great Britain and Ireland from 25 October 1760 until his death in 1820. The Acts of Union 1800 unified Great Britain and Ireland into the United Kingdom of Great Britain and Ireland, with George as its king. He was concurrently duke and prince-elector of Hanover in the Holy Roman Empire before becoming King of Hanover on 12 October 1814.

entries.name: Charlotte Brontë
entries.desc: Charlotte Nicholls, commonly known by her maiden name Charlotte Brontë, was an English novelist and poet, and was the elder sister of Emily, Anne and Branwell Brontë. She is best known for her nove…
entries.tldr: Charlotte Nicholls, commonly known by her maiden name Charlotte Brontë, was an English novelist and poet, and was the elder sister of Emily, Anne and Branwell Brontë. She is best known for her novel Jane Eyre, which was first published under the pseudonym Currer Bell. Jane Eyre was a great success on publication, and has since been acknowledged as a classic of English literature.

entries.name: José Echegaray
entries.desc: José Echegaray y Eizaguirre was a Spanish civil engineer, mathematician, statesman, and one of the leading Spanish dramatists of the last quarter of the 19th century. He was awarded the 1904 Nobel …
entries.tldr: José Echegaray y Eizaguirre was a Spanish civil engineer, mathematician, statesman, and one of the leading Spanish dramatists of the last quarter of the 19th century. He was awarded the 1904 Nobel Prize in Literature “in recognition of the numerous and brilliant compositions which, in an individual and original manner, have revived the great traditions of the Spanish drama”.

He served as the last bishop of the Unity of the Brethren before becoming a religious refugee and one of the earliest champions of universal education, a concept eventually set forth in his book Didactica Magna. As an educator and theologian, he led schools and advised governme…

entries.name: Sixtus II
entries.desc: Pope Sixtus II, also written as Pope Xystus II, was the bishop of Rome from 31 August 257 until his death on 6 August 258. He was killed along with seven deacons, including Lawrence of Rome, during…
entries.tldr: Pope Sixtus II, also written as Pope Xystus II, was the bishop of Rome from 31 August 257 until his death on 6 August 258. He was killed along with seven deacons, including Lawrence of Rome, during the persecution of Christians by the Emperor Valerian.

entries.name: Moshe ben Maimon

entries.tldr: Skandagupta was a Gupta Emperor who ruled in northern India. His Bhitari pillar inscription suggests that he restored the Gupta power by defeating his enemies, who may have been rebels or foreign invaders. He repulsed an invasion by the Indo-Hephthalites, probably the Kidarites.

The Bering Str…

entries.name: Norman Lockyer
entries.desc: Sir Joseph Norman Lockyer was an English scientist and astronomer. Along with the French scientist Pierre Janssen, he is credited with discovering the gas helium.
entries.tldr: Sir Joseph Norman Lockyer was an English scientist and astronomer. Along with the French scientist Pierre Janssen, he is credited with discovering the gas helium. Lockyer also is remembered for being the founder and first editor of the influential journal Nature.

entries.name: Pius VI
entries.desc: Pope Pius VI was head of the Catholic Church and ruler of the Papal States from 15 February 1775 to his death in August 1799.

entries.name: Radoje Domanović
entries.desc: Radoje Domanović was a Serbian journalist, writer and teacher, most famous for his satirical short stories. His adult years were a constant fight against tuberculosis.
entries.tldr: Radoje Domanović was a Serbian journalist, writer and teacher, most famous for his satirical short stories. His adult years were a constant fight against tuberculosis. This circumstance of his life, and the affection which he inspired in all who knew him, created an aura of romanticism and sentimentality which stand in contrast to his literary accomplishments as a satirist and a powerful critic…

entries.tldr: Shote Galica was a Kachak Albanian insurgent. She has been declared a People's Heroine of Albania.

entries.name: Abu Mansur al-Maturidi
entries.desc: Imam Abu Mansur al-Maturidi was a Hanafi jurist and theologian who is the eponym of the Maturidi school of kalam in Sunnism. He got his nisba from Māturīd, a district in Samarkand.
entries.tldr: Imam Abu Mansur al-Maturidi was a Hanafi jurist and theologian who is the eponym of the Maturidi school of kalam in Sunnism. He got his nisba from Māturīd, a district in Samarkand. His works include Tafsir al-Maturidi, a classic exegesis of the Qur'an, and Kitab al-Tawhid.

entries.name: Al-Walid II
entries.desc: Al-Walid ibn Yazid ibn Abd al-Malik, commonly known as al-Walid II, was the eleventh Umayyad caliph, ruling from 743 until his assassination in 744. He succeeded his uncle, Hisham ibn Abd al-Malik.
entries.tldr: Al-Walid ibn Yazid ibn Abd al-Malik, commonly known as al-Walid II, was the eleventh Umayyad caliph, ruling from 743 until his assassination in 744. He succeeded his uncle, Hisham ibn Abd al-Malik. He has the distinction of being the last Umayyad caliph to receive broad recognition across the empire; none of his successors—Yazid ibn al-Walid, who deposed him in 744, Ibrahim ibn al-Walid, and Ma…

entries.name: Hisham ibn Abd al-Malik
entries.desc: Hisham ibn Abd al-Malik ibn Marwan was the tenth Umayyad caliph, ruling from 724 until his death in 743. His grandson Abd al-Rahman I was the founder and first emir of the Emirate of Córdoba.
entries.tldr: Hisham ibn Abd al-Malik ibn Marwan was the tenth Umayyad caliph, ruling from 724 until his death in 743. His grandson Abd al-Rahman I was the founder and first emir of the Emirate of Córdoba.

entries.name: Marwan II
entries.desc: Marwan ibn Muhammad ibn Marwan, commonly known as Marwan II and surnamed al-Himar, was the fourteenth and last caliph of the Umayyad Caliphate, ruling from 744 until his death. His reign was domina…
entries.tldr: Marwan ibn Muhammad ibn Marwan, commonly known as Marwan II and surnamed al-Himar, was the fourteenth and last caliph of the Umayyad Caliphate, ruling from 744 until his death. His reign was dominated by a civil war, and he was the last Umayyad ruler to rule the united Caliphate before the Abbasid Revolution toppled the Umayyad dynasty.

entries.name: Yazid III
entries.desc: Yazid ibn al-Walid ibn Abd al-Malik, commonly known as Yazid III, was the twelfth Umayyad caliph, ruling from 744 until his death months later.
entries.tldr: Yazid ibn al-Walid ibn Abd al-Malik, commonly known as Yazid III, was the twelfth Umayyad caliph, ruling from 744 until his death months later.

entries.name: Charles Perrault
entries.desc: Charles Perrault was a French author and member of the Académie Française. He laid the foundations for a new literary genre, the fairy tale, with his works derived from earlier folk tales, publishe…
entries.tldr: Charles Perrault was a French author and member of the Académie Française. He laid the foundations for a new literary genre, the fairy tale, with his works derived from earlier folk tales, published in his 1697 book Histoires ou contes du temps passé. The best known of his tales include "Little Red Riding Hood", "Cinderella", "Puss in Boots", "Sleeping Beauty", and "Bluebeard".

entries.name: Ferdinand II of Aragon
entries.desc: Ferdinand II was King of Aragon from 1479 until his death in 1516. As the husband and co-ruler of Queen Isabella I of Castile, he was also King of Castile from 1475 to 1504.
entries.tldr: Ferdinand II was King of Aragon from 1479 until his death in 1516. As the husband and co-ruler of Queen Isabella I of Castile, he was also King of Castile from 1475 to 1504. He reigned jointly with Isabella over a dynastically unified Spain; together, they are known as the Catholic Monarchs.

entries.name: Richard Bedford Bennett
entries.desc: Richard Bedford Bennett, 1st Viscount Bennett was a Canadian lawyer, businessman, philanthropist, and politician who served as the 11th prime minister of Canada from 1930 to 1935.
entries.tldr: Richard Bedford Bennett, 1st Viscount Bennett was a Canadian lawyer, businessman, philanthropist, and politician who served as the 11th prime minister of Canada from 1930 to 1935.

entries.name: Robert Borden
entries.desc: Sir Robert Laird Borden was a Canadian lawyer and politician who served as the eighth prime minister of Canada from 1911 to 1920. He is best known for his leadership of Canada during World War I.
entries.tldr: Sir Robert Laird Borden was a Canadian lawyer and politician who served as the eighth prime minister of Canada from 1911 to 1920. He is best known for his leadership of Canada during World War I.

entries.name: Wilfrid Laurier
entries.desc: Sir Henri Charles Wilfrid Laurier was the seventh prime minister of Canada from 1896 to 1911. The first French Canadian prime minister, his 15-year tenure remains the longest uninterrupted term of …
entries.tldr: Sir Henri Charles Wilfrid Laurier was the seventh prime minister of Canada from 1896 to 1911. The first French Canadian prime minister, his 15-year tenure remains the longest uninterrupted term of office among Canadian prime ministers and his nearly 45 years of service in the House of Commons is a record for the House. Laurier is best known for his compromises between English and French Canada.

entries.name: Charles Tupper
entries.desc: Sir Charles Tupper, 1st Baronet, was a Canadian Father of Confederation who served as the sixth prime minister of Canada from May 1 to July 8, 1896.
entries.tldr: Sir Charles Tupper, 1st Baronet, was a Canadian Father of Confederation who served as the sixth prime minister of Canada from May 1 to July 8, 1896. As the premier of Nova Scotia from 1864 to 1867, he led Nova Scotia into Confederation.

entries.name: Mackenzie Bowell
entries.desc: Sir Mackenzie Bowell was the fifth prime minister of Canada, serving from 1894 to 1896.
entries.tldr: Sir Mackenzie Bowell was the fifth prime minister of Canada, serving from 1894 to 1896.

entries.name: John Sparrow David Thompson
entries.desc: Sir John Sparrow David Thompson was the fourth prime minister of Canada, serving from 1892 until his death in 1894.
entries.tldr: Sir John Sparrow David Thompson was the fourth prime minister of Canada, serving from 1892 until his death in 1894. He had previously been fifth premier of Nova Scotia for a brief period in 1882. He is the only post-Confederation provincial premier to become prime minister, as of 2026.

entries.name: John Abbott
entries.desc: Sir John Joseph Caldwell Abbott was the third prime minister of Canada, serving from 1891 to 1892. He held office as the leader of the Conservative Party.
entries.tldr: Sir John Joseph Caldwell Abbott was the third prime minister of Canada, serving from 1891 to 1892. He held office as the leader of the Conservative Party.

entries.name: Urraca of León
entries.desc: Urraca, called the Reckless (La Temeraria), was Queen of León, Castile and Galicia from 1109 until her death. She is considered the first European queen to reign in her own right.
entries.tldr: Urraca, called the Reckless (La Temeraria), was Queen of León, Castile and Galicia from 1109 until her death. She claimed the imperial title as suo jure Empress of All Spain and Empress of All Galicia. She is considered to be the first European queen to reign in her own right.

entries.name: John A. Macdonald
entries.desc: Sir John Alexander Macdonald was the first prime minister of Canada, serving from 1867 to 1873 and from 1878 until his death in 1891. He was the dominant figure of Canadian Confederation.
entries.tldr: Sir John Alexander Macdonald was the first prime minister of Canada, serving from 1867 to 1873 and from 1878 until his death in 1891. He was the dominant figure of Canadian Confederation, and had a political career that spanned almost half a century.

entries.name: Alexander Mackenzie
entries.desc: Alexander Mackenzie (1822–1892) was the second prime minister of Canada, serving from 1873 to 1878. A journalist and stonemason by trade, he was the first Liberal PM of Canada.
entries.tldr: Alexander Mackenzie (1822–1892) was the second prime minister of Canada. He began his career as a stonemason and journalist, entering politics in the 1860s and becoming Liberal Party leader. His government introduced the secret ballot and the Supreme Court of Canada.

entries.name: Stanislao Cannizzaro
entries.desc: Stanislao Cannizzaro was an Italian chemist. He is famous for the Cannizzaro reaction and for his influential role in the atomic-weight deliberations of the Karlsruhe Congress in 1860.
entries.tldr: Stanislao Cannizzaro was an Italian chemist. He is famous for the Cannizzaro reaction and for his influential role in the atomic-weight deliberations of the Karlsruhe Congress in 1860.

entries.name: John Ambrose Fleming
entries.desc: Sir John Ambrose Fleming was a British electrical engineer and physicist who invented the thermionic valve (vacuum tube) and established the right-hand rule in physics.
entries.tldr: Sir John Ambrose Fleming was a British electrical engineer and physicist. He is known for inventing the vacuum tube radio transmitter—with which the first transatlantic radio transmission was made—and establishing the right-hand rule used in physics.

entries.name: Alexander Bestuzhev
entries.desc: Alexander Alexandrovich Bestuzhev was a Russian writer and Decembrist who, after the failed revolt, was exiled to the Caucasus where he wrote romantic fiction under the pseudonym Marlinsky.
entries.tldr: Alexander Alexandrovich Bestuzhev was a Russian writer and Decembrist. After the Decembrist revolt he was sent into exile to the Caucasus where the Russian Empire was waging war against the Circassians. There writing under the pseudonym Marlinsky he became known as a romantic poet, short story writer and novelist.

entries.name: William Pitt the Younger
entries.desc: William Pitt was a British statesman who served as both the last prime minister of Great Britain and the first prime minister of the United Kingdom, holding office twice between 1783 and 1806.
entries.tldr: William Pitt was a British statesman who served as the last prime minister of Great Britain from 1783 until the Acts of Union 1800, and the first official prime minister of the United Kingdom from January 1801. He left office in March 1801, but served as prime minister again from 1804 until his death in 1806. He was also Chancellor of the Exchequer for all his time as prime minister.

entries.name: Hussein bin Ali
entries.desc: Hussein bin Ali was Sharif and Emir of Mecca and King of the Hejaz, who launched the Arab Revolt against the Ottoman Empire in 1916 during World War I.
entries.tldr: Hussein bin Ali was the Sharif of Mecca from 1908 and King of the Hejaz from 1916 to 1924. He launched the Arab Revolt against Ottoman rule during World War I, with British backing. He sought to establish a unified Arab state but was eventually driven from Arabia by Ibn Saud in 1924.

entries.name: Pierre Joseph Bonnaterre
entries.desc: Abbé Pierre Joseph Bonnaterre was a French zoologist who contributed to the Tableau encyclopédique et méthodique and was the first scientist to study the feral child Victor of Aveyron.
entries.tldr: Abbé Pierre Joseph Bonnaterre was a French zoologist who contributed sections on cetaceans, mammals, birds, reptiles, amphibians, fish, and insects to the Tableau encyclopédique et méthodique. He is also notable as the first scientist to study the feral child Victor of Aveyron.

entries.name: Blaise Pascal
entries.desc: Blaise Pascal was a French mathematician, physicist, inventor, philosopher, and Catholic writer whose work spans projective geometry, probability theory, and Christian apologetics.
entries.tldr: Blaise Pascal was a French mathematician, physicist, inventor, philosopher, and Catholic writer. He made major contributions to projective geometry, probability theory, and the design of mechanical calculators. His Pensées remains a landmark of Christian apologetics.

entries.name: Xerxes I
entries.desc: Xerxes I was the fourth King of Kings of the Achaemenid Empire, reigning from 486 BC until his assassination in 465 BC, known for his massive invasion of Greece.
entries.tldr: Xerxes I was a Persian ruler who reigned as the fourth King of Kings of the Achaemenid Empire, reigning from 486 BC until his assassination in 465 BC. He was the son of Darius the Great and Atossa, a daughter of Cyrus the Great.

entries.name: Verner von Heidenstam
entries.desc: Carl Gustaf Verner von Heidenstam was a Swedish poet and novelist who won the 1916 Nobel Prize in Literature, celebrated for his lyrical evocations of Swedish history and landscape.
entries.tldr: Carl Gustaf Verner von Heidenstam was a Swedish poet, novelist and laureate of the 1916 Nobel Prize in Literature. He was a member of the Swedish Academy from 1912. His poems and prose work are filled with a great joy of life, sometimes imbued with a love of Swedish history and scenery, particularly its physical aspects.

entries.name: María de las Mercedes, Princess of Asturias
entries.desc: María de las Mercedes was the eldest child of King Alfonso XII of Spain and heiress presumptive to the Spanish throne, holding the title Princess of Asturias for all 24 years of her life.
entries.tldr: María de las Mercedes, Princess of Asturias was the eldest child of King Alfonso XII of Spain and his second wife, Maria Christina of Austria. She was Princess of Asturias, the heiress presumptive to the Crown of Spain, for all 24 years of her life.

entries.name: Nikolai Ivanovich Lobachevsky
entries.desc: Nikolai Ivanovich Lobachevsky was a Russian mathematician who pioneered hyperbolic geometry, challenging Euclidean axioms and laying groundwork for non-Euclidean mathematics.
entries.tldr: Nikolai Ivanovich Lobachevsky was a Russian mathematician and geometer, known primarily for his work on hyperbolic geometry, otherwise known as Lobachevskian geometry, and also for his fundamental study on Dirichlet integrals, known as the Lobachevsky integral formula.

entries.name: Hatshepsut
entries.desc: Hatshepsut was the sixth pharaoh of the Eighteenth Dynasty of Egypt, reigning as queen regnant from c. 1479 BC and conducting major trade expeditions to Punt and ambitious building programs.
entries.tldr: Hatshepsut was the sixth pharaoh of the Eighteenth Dynasty of Egypt, ruling first as regent, then as queen regnant from c. 1479 BC until c. 1458 BC and the Great Royal Wife of Pharaoh Thutmose II.

entries.name: Edward III of England
entries.desc: Edward III was King of England from 1327 to 1377, known for his military success and for transforming England into one of Europe's most formidable military powers.
entries.tldr: Edward III was King of England from January 1327 until his death in 1377. He is noted for his military success and for restoring royal authority after the disastrous reign of his father, Edward II. Edward III transformed the Kingdom of England into one of the most formidable military powers in Europe. He is also credited with founding the Order of the Garter and initiating the Hundred Years' War with France.

entries.name: John, King of England
entries.desc: John was King of England from 1199 to 1216, whose reign saw the sealing of Magna Carta and the collapse of the Angevin Empire in France.
entries.tldr: John was King of England from 1199 until his death in 1216. He lost the Duchy of Normandy and most of his other French lands to King Philip II of France, resulting in the collapse of the Angevin Empire and contributing to the subsequent growth in power of the French Capetian dynasty during the 13th century. The baronial revolt at the end of John's reign led to the sealing of Magna Carta, a document of great historical importance in the development of constitutional law.

entries.name: Étienne Lenoir
entries.desc: Jean Joseph Étienne Lenoir was a Belgian-French engineer who invented the internal combustion engine in 1858.
entries.tldr: Jean Joseph Étienne Lenoir was a Belgian-French engineer who invented the internal combustion engine in 1858. Prior designs for such engines were patented as early as 1807 and 1854. His engine used illuminating gas and was the first to be produced commercially, with several hundred built before 1865.

entries.name: James Barry
entries.desc: James Barry was a British military surgeon and Inspector General of Hospitals, one of the first women to practise medicine in Britain under a male identity.
entries.tldr: James Barry was a British military surgeon who rose to the rank of Inspector General of Hospitals. Born Margaret Ann Bulkley, Barry lived as a man for decades, becoming one of the first women to practise medicine in Britain. Barry performed one of the earliest successful caesarean sections and was renowned for improving conditions for soldiers and the poor in colonial postings across the British Empire.

entries.name: Leopold II of Belgium
entries.desc: Leopold II was the second king of the Belgians from 1865 to 1909, and the founder and sole owner of the Congo Free State, infamous for brutal exploitation of its people.
entries.tldr: Leopold II was the second king of the Belgians from 1865 to 1909, and the founder and sole owner of the Congo Free State from 1885 to 1908. His regime in the Congo was responsible for the deaths of millions through forced labour and atrocities. International pressure eventually forced him to cede the territory to Belgium in 1908.

entries.name: Leopold I of Belgium
entries.desc: Leopold I was the first king of the Belgians, reigning from 1831 until his death in 1865 after being chosen by the Belgian National Congress.
entries.tldr: Leopold I was the first king of the Belgians, reigning from 21 July 1831 until his death in 1865. He had previously served as a general in Russian service during the Napoleonic Wars. After declining the Greek throne, he accepted the Belgian crown and steered the new state through its early years with skill, strengthening its constitutional monarchy.

entries.name: Xenophon
entries.desc: Xenophon of Athens was a Greek military leader, historian, and philosopher, best known for leading the Ten Thousand mercenaries back to Greece after their stranding deep in Persia.
entries.tldr: Xenophon of Athens was a Greek military leader, philosopher, and historian. At the age of 30, he was elected as one of the leaders of the retreating Greek mercenaries, the Ten Thousand, who had been part of Cyrus the Younger's attempt to seize control of the Achaemenid Empire. His account of this retreat, the Anabasis, became one of the most influential military memoirs of antiquity. He also wrote Socratic dialogues and histories of Greece.

entries.name: Empress Amélie of Brazil
entries.desc: Amélie of Leuchtenberg was Empress of Brazil as the wife of Pedro I, the first Emperor of Brazil.
entries.tldr: Amélie of Leuchtenberg was Empress of Brazil as the wife of Pedro I of Brazil. She was the daughter of Eugène de Beauharnais, stepson of Napoleon I. She accompanied Pedro I during his abdication and followed him to Portugal where he fought for his daughter Maria II's rights. She was noted for her charitable work and dignity during political upheaval.

entries.name: Francis I of France
entries.desc: Francis I was King of France from 1515 to 1547, a Renaissance patron who brought Italian artists and scholars to his court and waged long rivalry with Holy Roman Emperor Charles V.
entries.tldr: Francis I was King of France from 1515 until his death in 1547. He was the son of Charles, Count of Angoulême, and Louise of Savoy. He succeeded his first cousin once removed and father-in-law Louis XII. Francis was a key figure of the French Renaissance, patronising artists like Leonardo da Vinci and waging the Italian Wars against the Habsburgs.

entries.name: William III of England
entries.desc: William III, also known as William of Orange, was King of England, Scotland, and Ireland from 1689 and Dutch stadtholder, who led the Glorious Revolution.
entries.tldr: William III and II, also known as William of Orange, was the sovereign Prince of Orange from birth, Stadtholder of Holland, Zeeland, Utrecht, Guelders, and Overijssel in the Dutch Republic from 1672, and King of England, Ireland, and Scotland from 1689 until his death in 1702. He ruled jointly with his wife, Queen Mary II, until her death in 1694. His Glorious Revolution reshaped the British constitutional monarchy.

entries.name: Frederick II, Holy Roman Emperor
entries.desc: Frederick II was Holy Roman Emperor from 1220, King of Sicily, Germany, Jerusalem, and Italy — a polymath ruler known as Stupor Mundi, the wonder of the world.
entries.tldr: Frederick II was King of Sicily from 1198, King of Germany from 1212, King of Italy and Holy Roman Emperor from 1220, and King of Jerusalem from 1225 to 1228. He was the son of Emperor Henry VI of the Hohenstaufen dynasty and Queen Constance I of Sicily. A scholar in multiple languages, he wrote a landmark treatise on falconry and fostered a multicultural court in Palermo.

entries.name: Giovanni Losi
entries.desc: Giovanni Losi was an Italian Catholic priest and Comboni missionary who served in South Sudan, ministering under extreme hardship in Central Africa.
entries.tldr: Giovanni Losi M.C.C.I. was an Italian Catholic priest and member of the Comboni Missionaries of the Heart of Jesus. He spent the majority of his ministry in South Sudan, working in difficult conditions in Central Africa. He is venerated for his dedication to spreading Christianity and providing pastoral care in remote regions of Africa.

entries.name: Thomas Stamford Raffles
entries.desc: Sir Thomas Stamford Raffles was a British colonial official and naturalist who founded modern Singapore and served as governor in the Dutch East Indies.
entries.tldr: Sir Thomas Stamford Bingley Raffles was a British colonial official who served as the governor of the Dutch East Indies between 1811 and 1816 and lieutenant-governor of Bencoolen between 1818 and 1824. He founded modern Singapore in 1819. Raffles was also a keen naturalist who co-founded the Zoological Society of London and had many species named after him.

entries.name: Maria Zankovetska
entries.desc: Maria Zankovetska was a pioneering Ukrainian theater actress regarded as one of the greatest performers in Ukrainian theatrical history.
entries.tldr: Maria Kostiantynivna Adasovska, better known under her pseudonym Maria Zankovetska, was a Ukrainian theater actress. Some sources date her birth to 3 August 1860. She is regarded as the greatest actress in the history of Ukrainian theater and played leading roles in many Ukrainian dramatic productions during a period when Ukrainian culture faced significant suppression.

entries.name: Bartolomeu Dias
entries.desc: Bartolomeu Dias was a Portuguese explorer who in 1488 became the first European to round the southern tip of Africa, proving a sea route to Asia was possible.
entries.tldr: Bartolomeu Dias was a Portuguese mariner and explorer. In February 1488, he became the first European navigator to round the southern tip of Africa and to demonstrate that the most effective southward route for ships is in the open ocean, well to the west of the African coast. His discoveries were later used by Vasco da Gama to establish a sea route between Europe and Asia.

entries.name: Sophia of Nassau
entries.desc: Sophia of Nassau was Queen of Sweden and Norway as wife of King Oscar II, the longest-serving queen in Swedish history until 2011.
entries.tldr: Sophia of Nassau, also Sofia, was Queen of Sweden and Norway as the wife of King Oscar II. She was Queen of Sweden for 35 years, longer than anyone before her, and the longest-serving queen until 2011, when she was surpassed by Queen Silvia. She is also the most recent woman to have been officially Dowager Queen of Sweden.

entries.name: Louise of the Netherlands
entries.desc: Louise of the Netherlands, also called Lovisa, was Queen of Sweden and Norway from 1859 until her death in 1871 as wife of King Charles XV & IV.
entries.tldr: Louise of the Netherlands, also called Lovisa, was Queen of Sweden and Norway from 8 July 1859 until her death in 1871 as the wife of King Charles XV & IV. She was born a Dutch princess and became one of the most beloved queens of Sweden, known for her charm and grace at court. Her early death at age 42 was mourned widely across Scandinavia.

entries.name: Hieronymus Bosch
entries.desc: Hieronymus Bosch was a Dutch painter from Brabant known for his fantastical, moralistic imagery blending religious themes with nightmarish surreal scenes.
entries.tldr: Hieronymus Bosch was a Dutch painter from Brabant. He is one of the most notable representatives of the Early Netherlandish painting school. His work, generally oil on oak wood, mainly contains fantastic illustrations of religious concepts and narratives. His most famous work, The Garden of Earthly Delights, remains one of the most complex and debated triptychs in art history.

entries.name: George IV of the United Kingdom
entries.desc: George IV was King of the United Kingdom from 1820 to 1830, having previously served as Prince Regent during his father's mental illness from 1811.
entries.tldr: George IV was King of the United Kingdom of Great Britain and Ireland and King of Hanover from 29 January 1820 until his death in 1830. At the time of his accession to the throne, he was acting as prince regent for his father, King George III, having done so since 5 February 1811 during his father's final mental illness. He was known for his extravagance and his patronage of the arts and architecture.

entries.name: Romulus Augustus
entries.desc: Romulus Augustus, nicknamed Augustulus, was the last Roman Emperor of the West, deposed in 476 CE after a reign of just ten months.
entries.tldr: Romulus Augustus, nicknamed Augustulus, was Roman emperor of the West from 31 October 475 until 4 September 476. Romulus was placed on the imperial throne while still a minor by his father Orestes, the magister militum, for whom he served as little more than a figurehead. After a rule of ten months, the barbarian general Odoacer defeated and killed Orestes and deposed Romulus, an event traditionally marking the fall of the Western Roman Empire.
